# ISAC evaluations

## R1-2603925 - R1-2603925_Moderator (CATT)_Moderator summary #1 on ISAC evaluations.docx
Source: E:\标准相关资料\RAN1_125_ISAC_文档整理_20260616\RAN1_125_ISAC\01_Evaluations\Moderator_Summaries\R1-2603925_Moderator (CATT)_Moderator summary #1 on ISAC evaluations.docx

3GPP TSG RAN WG1 #125 R1-2603925

Dalian, China, May 18th – 22nd, 2026

Agenda item: 10.8.1

Source: Moderator (CATT)

Title: Moderator summary #1 on ISAC evaluations

Document for: Discussion/Decision

Introduction

In 6G Radio study, RAN1 is tasked to evaluate sensing performance and if necessary, extend channel modelling for the selected use cases [1]. The relevant scenarios and requirements can be found in [2]. Per RAN1 Chairman’s guidance, evaluation of sensing in this meeting is planned as follows.

[TABLE]
10.8.1 Evaluations Note 1: Including deployment scenarios, performance evaluation methodologies/metrics/KPIs for the target use cases, and potential extension of channel model, if necessary

The following email thread is assigned for this agenda:

[TABLE]
[125-R20-6GR-ISAC-Evaluations] Email discussion on Rel-20 6GR-ISAC-Evaluations–Yongqiang (CATT) To be used for sharing updates on online/offline schedule, details on what is to be discussed in online/offline sessions, tdoc number of the moderator summary for online session, etc.

This document summarizes the contributions and discussions on Agenda 10.8.1 (Evaluations) in this meeting.

Proposal marks

In this document, issues, questions and proposals are tagged and colored with the following rules.

The proposals in this document are tagged and color coded respectively

[H] Proposal: High Priority, to be handled firstly.

[M] Proposal: Medium Priority, which may be handled on best effort basis or with clear majority view.

[L] Proposal: Low Priority, which may be treated only if there is clear consensus.

In current round for discussion, issues to be discussed are further tagged with [FL1]. Note: In case there is more than one round discussion, some proposals without update are still marked with early round number. If any company hasn’t provided a comment yet, or prefer to update the comments, feel free to comment on them.

File uploading

When uploading draft files for email discussion, if needed, please follow the naming convention in this example:

ISACEvaFLS-v000-Moderator.docx

ISACEvaFLS-v001-Moderator-CompanyA.docx

ISACEvaFLS-v002-CompanyA-CompanyB.docx

ISACEvaFLS-v003-CompanyB-CompanyC.docx

If needed, you may “lock” a spreadsheet file for 30 minutes by creating a checkout file, as in this example:

Assume CompanyC wants to update ISACEvaFLS-v002-CompanyA-CompanyB.docx.

CompanyC uploads an empty file named ISACEvaFLS-v003-CompanyB-CompanyC.checkout

CompanyC checks that no one else has created a checkout file simultaneously, and if there is a collision, CompanyC tries to coordinate with the company who made the other checkout (see, e.g., contact list below).

CompanyC then has 30 minutes to upload ISACEvaFLS-v003-CompanyB-CompanyC.docx

If no update is uploaded in 30 minutes, other companies can ignore the checkout file.

Note that the file timestamps on the server are in UTC time.

Online proposals

Monday

N/A

Tuesday

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.1-1on

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, for the baseline cases/combinations, encourage companies to improve the sensing performance, e.g. by exploring different sensing modes.

Note: Evaluating any sensing mode in this agenda does not necessarily mean any RAN1 specification impact.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-1on

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, at least the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Evaluate the performance of sensing measurement/results, where the sensing measurement/result may be able to assist communication.

Scenario: Urban Grid

Sensing measurement/result to assist communication may include, e.g., delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the channel, information of reconstructed wall/building, etc. FFS details.

FFS detailed evaluation assumptions, metric, etc.

Up to company to evaluate and report communication performance assisted by sensing.

Company should at least use realistic sensing measurement/result to assist communication.

Company should use the same scenario and ISAC channel as sensing performance evaluation.

Up to company to report communication-related metric and the corresponding performance gain.

[FL1][H] Proposal 7.2-1on

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation, for tracking sensing targets/objects, consider the following trajectory model:

Baseline: Straight-line constant velocity trajectory

Other trajectories can also be reported by companies

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.1-1on

For object detection and/or tracking, update the definition of velocity accuracy for 6G ISAC evaluation:

Velocity accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated velocity and the corresponding true velocity of a sensing target. For single node TRP monostatic sensing or single pair of bistatic sensing, both the radial velocity accuracy and the 3D velocity accuracy can be estimated. The true radial velocity is the projection of true 3D velocity on the direction from TRP to target. the sum of the following two components:

Component 1: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to STX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the STX;

Component 2: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to SRX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the SRX.

Note: If STx and SRx are static, true radial velocity is , where is the true 3D velocity of the target, is the bistatic angle between incident direction and scattering direction, and is the angle between the true 3D velocity vector and the bisector vector within the bistatic angle . For mono-static sensing, .

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.2-1on

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, for object detection and/or tracking, regarding the performance target in UMa and Indoor Factory scenarios given by RAN Plenary:

The performance objective on False Alarm Probability (5%) applies to both False Alarm Probability Type 1 and False Alarm Probability Type 2.

The vertical localization accuracy requirement is only applicable to the case of sensing UAV.

Thursday

There is no offline on Monday.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.1-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, for the baseline cases/combinations, the following sensing modes are considered for the baseline cases/combinations.

[TABLE]
Sensing target | Indoor or outdoor | Scenario | Carrier frequency | Sensing mode
UAV | Outdoor | UMa-AV, SMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for UMa-AV only) | TRP mono-static, TRP-TRP bi-static, TRP-UE bi-static (UAV UE?)
Human | Outdoor | UMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static TRP-TRP bi-static
Human | Indoor | Indoor Hotspot | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static UE mono-static, TRP-TRP bi-static
Vehicle | Outdoor | Urban Grid, Highway | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for Urban Grid only) | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static, UE-UE bi-static, TRP mono-static
AGV | Indoor | Indoor factory (SH) | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static TRP mono-static

Note: Other sensing modes (including multi-static sensing) for a baseline case/combination are not precluded.

Note2: Evaluating any sensing mode in this agenda does not necessarily mean any RAN1 specification impact.

Note3: Sensing modes listed in the table may not have the best performance for baseline cases. Encourage companies to improve the performance, e.g. exploring different sensing modes.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, at least the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Evaluate the performance of sensing measurement/results, where the sensing measurement/result may be able to assist communication.

Scenario: Urban Grid with EO type-2

Sensing measurement/result to assist communication, may include, e.g. delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the channel, information of reconstructed wall/building, etc. FFS details.

FFS detailed evaluation assumptions, metric, etc.

Up to company to evaluate and report communication performance assisted by sensing.

Company should at least use realistic sensing measurement/result to assist communication.

Company should use the same scenario and ISAC channel as sensing performance evaluation.

Up to company to report communication-related metric and the corresponding performance gain.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-2a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, further discuss the following potential candidate approaches to assist communication:

Approach 1: Utilize sensing measurement/result to assist the communication performance.

Sensing measurement/result may include, e.g. delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the channel, information of reconstructed wall/building, etc.

Approach 2: Utilize sensing resource/RS to perform communication-related measurement, and then assist the communication performance.

E.g., Perform CSI/beam-related measurement on sensing resource/RS, to assist CSI acquisition and/or beam management.

[FL1][H] Proposal 7.2-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation, for tracking sensing targets/objects, consider the following trajectory model:

Baseline: Straight-line constant velocity trajectory

Other trajectories can also be reported by companies

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.1-1a

For object detection and/or tracking, update the definition of velocity accuracy for 6G ISAC evaluation:

Velocity accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated velocity and the corresponding true velocity of a sensing target. For single node TRP monostatic sensing or single pair of bistatic sensing, both the radial velocity accuracy and the 3D velocity accuracy can be estimated. The true radial velocity is the projection of true 3D velocity on the direction from TRP to target. the sum of the following two components:

Component 1: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to STX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the STX;

Component 2: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to SRX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the SRX.

Note: If STx and SRx are static, true radial velocity is , where is the true 3D velocity of the target, is the bistatic angle between incident direction and scattering direction, and is the angle between the true 3D velocity vector and the bisector vector within the bistatic angle . For mono-static sensing, .

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.2-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, for object detection and/or tracking, regarding the performance target in UMa and Indoor Factory scenarios given by RAN Plenary:

The performance objective on False Alarm Probability (5%) applies to both False Alarm Probability Type 1 and False Alarm Probability Type 2.

The vertical localization accuracy requirement is only applicable to the case of sensing UAV.

Scope of evaluation

The following working assumption was reached in RAN1#124bis [12]:

[TABLE]
Working Assumption For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, evaluate object detection and/or tracking for the following sensing targets/objects: UAV, human, vehicle, AGV discuss further on evaluation on communication assistance

Several companies propose to confirm the working assumption as it is, while some other companies additionally propose updates especially for communication assistance part.

[TABLE]
FL | At this stage, the most urgent task is to figure out the study direction. It is not urgent to confirm or modify the working assumption.

Baseline cases/combinations for object detection and/or tracking

For object detection and/or tracking, baseline cases/combinations were agreed in RAN1#124bis [12]:

[TABLE]
Agreement: For Rel-20 6G sensing performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, the following cases/combinations are considered as baseline cases/combinations. Note: sensing mode(s) for each row will be discussed Other cases/combinations can also be evaluated, reported and collected from companies. Note: In this agreement, no intention to de-prioritize other scenarios or carrier frequencies.

Note that, the sensing mode(s) for each baseline case/combination is TBD.

Note that, multi-static can be constructed by one or more sensing modes with more than one STx and/or SRx. It is not precluded anyway.

In addition, Nokia proposes to remove 30 GHz for UMa and SMa, and Ofinno would like to consider 30 GHz as optional. FL thinks individual companies can choose the evaluated frequency but no need to prohibit an agreed combination. Panasonic suggests mapping different baseline combinations into cases in TR 22.837 and TR 22.870. FL thinks this does not change our evaluation scope with or without the mapping. Some other companies would like to modify the table, e.g. adding scenarios. Please refrain from such up-scoping at this stage due to the limited time.

Finally, the following proposal is made, based on the input. Some notes are also added to (1) clarify the intension, and (2) keep the door open for other sensing modes.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.1-1

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, the following sensing modes are considered for the baseline cases/combinations.

[TABLE]
Sensing target | Indoor or outdoor | Scenario | Carrier frequency | Sensing mode
UAV | Outdoor | UMa-AV, SMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for UMa-AV only) | TRP mono-static, TRP-TRP bi-static
Human | Outdoor | UMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static
Human | Indoor | Indoor Hotspot | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static
Vehicle | Outdoor | Urban Grid, Highway | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for Urban Grid only) | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static
AGV | Indoor | Indoor factory (SH) | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static

Note: Other sensing modes (including multi-static sensing) for a baseline case/combination are not precluded.

Note2: Evaluating any sensing mode in this agenda does not necessarily mean any RAN1 specification impact.

[TABLE]
Company | Company | Yes or No | Yes or No | Comments
Tiami | Tiami | Yes, but | Yes, but | We would like to consider UAV, TRP-UE bi-static and Human TRP-TRP bi-static as well.
IDCC | IDCC | Comment | Comment | We would like TRP-UE bistatic added to UAV row. Considering mixed LOS/NLOS conditions and low altitude UAVs, UE involvement may be critical in meeting performance requirements. The rest looks good.
vivo | vivo | Yes | Yes | 
T-Mobile USA | T-Mobile USA | Ok | Ok | TRP-TRP bi(multi)static could be useful to evaluate in some outdoor scenarios, but generally we are fine with the majority.
OPPO | OPPO | Yes on Human/Vehicle and AGV | Yes on Human/Vehicle and AGV | We do not see the reason to exclude UE involvement for UAV sensing, especially given: We show in our contribution that the probability for TRP-monostatic to be in LOS and the probability for TRP-ground UE bistatic to be in LOS+LOS are comparable. The UE in TRP-UE or UE-TRP bistatic sensing can be aerial UE, as supported by 38.901 ISAC channel model.
Cohere | Cohere | Yea | Yea | 
ZTE | ZTE | Comment | Comment | We would like to consider TRP mono-static in Vehicle scenario as well, especially for highway scenario. TRP should be very important sensing device around the road which could provide satisfying LOS conditions.
Ericsson | Ericsson | Yes | Yes | 
Qualcomm | Qualcomm | No | No | We don’t agree with the effort to explicitly limit to specific sensing modes the evaluation and we believe this table will be used out of context for downscoping specific sensing modes. We also observe that TRP-UE has been included in multiple outdoor and indoor scenarios. What will we learn additionally by having the same sensing mode in multiple scenarios? TRP-UE and UE-TRP have a lot of similarities; mainly the Tx power is different; the rest are the same. Instead of having both modes everywhere, at least for one of scenarios UE monostatic should be included. In the above list, it appears as if the TRP-UE/UE-TRP are the most important scenarios, however, from our understanding, these sensing mode suffers the most from bad LOS condition and will have limited performance in many cases. TRP-based sensing modes or UE-monostatic will have much better LOS conditions in several of these deployments.
Samsung | Samsung | No | No | UE monostatic should be included in AGV, at least. For remaining sensing mode (e.g., UE-UE bistatic), at least one scenario needs to be covered it.
Google | Google | Yes | Yes | In general, we want to focus on cooperative and multi-static sensing architectures (specifically TRP-to-TRP, TRP-to-UE, and UE-to-TRP). We prefer to deprioritize any UE mono-static sensing in this release
Xiaomi | Xiaomi | [Yes] | [Yes] | Fine with the direction of the proposal. However, the RAN plenary has decided to study all 6 sensing modes, UE mono-static and UE-UE bistatic should then also be evaluated in at least one scenario, for example, InH and Urban Grid.
NEC | NEC | Yes | Yes | We would like to consider the UE involvement modes for UAV as well.
DCM | DCM |  |  | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static appear in multiple scenarios. For diversity, we think that TRP mono-static, TRP-TRP bi-static should be added to one more outdoor scenario e.g., Vehicle.
Huawei, HiSilicon | Huawei, HiSilicon | ok | ok | We sympathize the intention of listing the sensing modes for each scenario but in general we should avoid spending much time to arguing for adding more other sensing modes since other sensing modes are not precluded anyway.
Apple | Apple | No | No | UE monostatic should be at least one case e.g. Case3: Sensing Human in Indoor Hotspot. As stated, this implicitly down-scopes the UE monostatic use case.
PCL | PCL | Yes | Yes | We support the FL proposal.
CMCC | CMCC | Yes | Yes | 
ETRI | ETRI | See Comment | See Comment | Please consider TRP-UE bi-static for UAV sensing as well
Sony | Sony |  |  | TRP mono-static and TRP-TRP bistatic should be also supported in human outdoor scenarios and vehicles.
LGE | LGE | Yes, but | Yes, but | We agree in general. However, for indoor scenarios, TRP mono-static sensing may also be considered, since TRP-target and target-UE distances can be comparable while TRPs can provide higher transmit power and coverage benefits.
NIST | NIST | Comment | Comment | The evaluation should cover all 6 sensing modes. For Urban/Grid, we would like UE monostatic and UE-UE bistatic configurations. The UEs can be RSU-type UEs.
Nokia | Nokia | No | No | We think TRP monostatic and TRP-TRP bistatic can be starting point for the evaluation of the use cases, while TRP-UE, UE-TRP bistatic should be firstly discuss in detail on UE types, configuration and capability of UE, then evaluated as second priority.
Spreadtrum | Spreadtrum | Yes | Yes | 
Panasonic | Panasonic | Yes | Yes | 
CATT/CICTCI | No | No | At least for Urban Grid scenario, we do not see UE-TRP bi-static and TRP-UE bi-static sensing modes can be considered as a good starting point for evaluation, due to the low LOS probability between TRP and vehicle as we commented in the last meeting, which has a significantly negative impact on the sensing performance. And we also provide initial simulation results for highway and urban grid scenario, as cited below, which shows that UE-UE bistatic sensing and UE monostatic sensing are more suitable for urban grid scenario. and also have comparable performance to the TRP-UE bistatic and UE-TRP bistatic sensing for highway scenario. Table 3: Evaluation results for RMa-Highway[R1-2603924] Table 4: Evaluation results for Urban grid[R1-2603924] For human outdoor sensing, similar issues are suffered since same LOS/NLOS formulas are used for TRP-target link in UMa scenario and urban grid scenario. Therefore, we propose to consider UE-UE bistatic sensing and UE mono-static sensing as baseline sensing mode for automotive sensing and human outdoor sensing. | At least for Urban Grid scenario, we do not see UE-TRP bi-static and TRP-UE bi-static sensing modes can be considered as a good starting point for evaluation, due to the low LOS probability between TRP and vehicle as we commented in the last meeting, which has a significantly negative impact on the sensing performance. And we also provide initial simulation results for highway and urban grid scenario, as cited below, which shows that UE-UE bistatic sensing and UE monostatic sensing are more suitable for urban grid scenario. and also have comparable performance to the TRP-UE bistatic and UE-TRP bistatic sensing for highway scenario. Table 3: Evaluation results for RMa-Highway[R1-2603924] Table 4: Evaluation results for Urban grid[R1-2603924] For human outdoor sensing, similar issues are suffered since same LOS/NLOS formulas are used for TRP-target link in UMa scenario and urban grid scenario. Therefore, we propose to consider UE-UE bistatic sensing and UE mono-static sensing as baseline sensing mode for automotive sensing and human outdoor sensing.

FL: Based on the input, the following proposal is made for Tuesday offline discussion.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.1-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, the following sensing modes are considered for the baseline cases/combinations.

[TABLE]
Sensing target | Indoor or outdoor | Scenario | Carrier frequency | Sensing mode
UAV | Outdoor | UMa-AV, SMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for UMa-AV only) | TRP mono-static, TRP-TRP bi-static, TRP-UE bi-static
Human | Outdoor | UMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static TRP-TRP bi-static
Human | Indoor | Indoor Hotspot | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static UE mono-static
Vehicle | Outdoor | Urban Grid, Highway | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for Urban Grid only) | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static, UE-UE bi-static
AGV | Indoor | Indoor factory (SH) | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static TRP mono-static

Note: Other sensing modes (including multi-static sensing) for a baseline case/combination are not precluded.

Note2: Evaluating any sensing mode in this agenda does not necessarily mean any RAN1 specification impact.

Communication assistance

Views on communication assistance from companies in RAN1#125 are summarized below.

Necessity of evaluating communication assistance

Evaluate sensing (e.g. environment, blocker, target) only: CMCC (1st), Samsung, Huawei, DOCOMO, Ericsson, Ofinno, [Panasonic],

Evaluate communication only (i.e. assuming ideal sensing result): vivo, ZTE, AT&T, Qualcomm,

Evaluate both sensing and communication: CMCC (2nd), AT&T, [Qualcomm], LGE, Google, Apple, T-Mobile USA, SK Telecom,

Up to companies report: xiaomi, Samsung (communication part), DOCOMO (communication part)

Deprioritize: Interdigital, ETRI, [LGE], Sony,

Use cases of communication assistance for evaluation

[TABLE]
Use case | What to sense | What/how to assist | Companies
Not limited | Detected channel multipath where wall/building exist nearby | Extract and reconstruct channel information, to generate hypothetical channel for communication purpose. Detailed usage is not limited. | Huawei
Not limited | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) | Up to vendors/operators. Detailed usage is not limited. | DOCOMO, Samsung, LGE, Ericsson, [Panasonic],
Beam management | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) | Assist beam prediction By using multipath channel information (e.g. angle, delay, power) of a UE from reconstructed wall/building By pre-identify static blocker such as wall/building | Vivo*, CMCC, [AT&T], Qualcomm, Google, T-Mobile USA, Sony, Lenovo, Tejas, NEC, China Telecom, Ofinno,
Beam management | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) | Assist beam forming Buildings are treated as virtual transmitters, where reflected or scattered paths are intentionally beamformed to reach NLOS UE | LGE, NIST,
Beam management | Blockers (e.g. vehicle) | Assis beam prediction By pre-identify dynamic blocker such as vehicle | CMCC, Apple, China Telecom,
Beam management | Human that holding the UE | Assist beam management By pre-acquiring LOS/NLOS, distance/delay from TRP to UE, channel Doppler, etc. | Nokia, Lenovo, China Telecom,
Beam management | None | Assist beam selection By using sensing resource to measure L1-RSRP and select communication beams | ZTE
CSI acquisition/prediction | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) | Assist time/frequency/spatial domain CSI acquisition/prediction By using dominant multipath channel information (e.g. angle, delay) from sensed environment | CMCC, [AT&T], Qualcomm, Google, Sony, Ofinno,
CSI acquisition/prediction | Blockers (e.g. vehicle) | Assist time/frequency/spatial domain CSI acquisition/prediction By using dominant multipath channel information (e.g. angle, delay) from sensed environment | CMCC, LGE, Google,
CSI acquisition/prediction | Human that holding the UE | Assist CSI acquisition By pre-acquiring LOS/NLOS, distance/delay from TRP to UE, channel Doppler, etc. | Nokia
CSI acquisition/prediction | None | Assist time domain CSI prediction By using multipath channel information (e.g. Doppler, etc.) achieved from sensing resource | Vivo
CSI acquisition/prediction | None | Assist CSI acquisition By measuring CSI from sensing resource | ZTE
BS/UE power saving | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) Blockers (e.g. vehicle) | Assist sleeping cycles and Tx/Rx operation By predicting deep fading periods with the reconstructed wall/building | [CMCC] , [T-Mobile USA],
Mobility management | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) Blockers (e.g. vehicle) | Assist handover reliability By using target cell's channel conditions based on the UE's trajectory and the reconstructed wall/building | LGE, [CMCC], [Qualcomm], T-Mobile USA, Sony, Lenovo, NEC, China Telecom, Ofinno,
Positioning enhancement | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) Blockers (e.g. vehicle) | Assist positioning accuracy By providing reliable anchors for NLOS mitigation and multipath-assisted positioning, from reconstructed wall/building | [CMCC], [Qualcomm]
Scheduling optimization/Link adaption | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) | Assist scheduling, e.g. resource allocation, spatial nulling, etc. Optimize time/frequency/MCS/beam… by using the reconstructed wall/building and UE’s location | LGE, AT&T, T-Mobile USA, NEC, China Telecom, Ofinno,
Scheduling optimization/Link adaption | Human that holding the UE | Assist scheduling, By observing UE’s mobility, blockage condition, and potential link LOS/NLOS identification or pathloss status | Lenovo, China Telecom,

Evaluation methodology

Proponents (e.g. Huawei, vivo, ZTE, Qualcomm, Apple, T-Mobile USA, Lenovo, NEC, etc.) propose updated methodologies in different ways explicitly or implicitly. And, depending on ‘how to assist communication’, different methodologies may appear (well summarized by Lenovo, see R1-2604242).

Approach 1: Assist communication by Prior information of Environment

An example from Huawei is copied below (slightly modified). Note that no sensing target (e.g. vehicle) is needed in this example.

[TABLE]
The high-level evaluation processing flow can be diagramed as follows: Deployment and scenario: Set up the simulation configuration, including the layout, sensing Tx/Rx node, and channel model. Sensing measurement: Perform sensing measurement and sensing signal processing to acquire the sensing measurement results. For an instance, the detected paths are extracted. Channel hypothesizing: Reconstruct the channel information e.g., the RSRP and multi-path component based on the sensing measurement results. For example, based on the sensing measurement results, the propagation channel can be regenerated by the same channel model and hypothesized for the Tx-Rx link. Communication assistance: Perform the assisting communication procedure and the details depend on the use cases to be assisted.

Approach 2: Assist communication by sensing the attached object (e.g. human) of UE

Lenovo: New methodology may include: define co-location of a passive target and a dropped UE according to the UE type

Approach 3: Assist communication by using sensing signal/resource

Lenovo: New methodology may include: define communication evaluation setup given sensing signal transmissions

Evaluation scenario

Urban grid: CMCC, vivo, Huawei, ZTE, Samsung, [DOCOMO], Ericsson

UMa: vivo

Depending on methodology: Lenovo

Others

No simultaneous/mixed sensing + communication evaluation: Ericsson, CATT/CICTCI,

Further discuss: Nokia, SK Telecom, Apple

Summary

FL: Companies’ views are SO Divergent on Everything😄. Having said this, we need to figure out what can we do and whether/what/how to do, with limited time.

Let’s start discussion to have better understanding with each other from high-level. Further details, like use case, metric, performance target, channel model extension… can be discussed later once the evaluation scope is clear.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-1

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, further discuss the following potential candidate SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scopes:

SAC Scope 0: No dedicated evaluation for communication assistance.

Note: Scope 0 can be considered as special case of Scope 1, assuming the communication can be assisted by sensing moving objects like vehicle, etc., which is already included in the study of (moving) object detection and/or tracking.

SAC Scope 1: Only evaluate sensing, which can potentially assist communication.

SAC Scope 1a: Evaluate sensing which can potentially assist communication. Evaluation and report of any communication performance or performance gain is up to companies.

SAC Scope 2: Evaluate communication performance with ideal sensing assistance.

SAC Scope 2a: Evaluate communication performance gain between ‘with ideal sensing assistance’ and ‘without any sensing assistance’.

SAC Scope 3: Evaluate communication performance with realistic sensing assistance.

SAC Scope 3a: Evaluate communication performance gain between ‘with realistic sensing assistance’ and ‘without any sensing assistance’.

SAC Scope 3b: Evaluate communication performance gain and loss between ‘with realistic sensing assistance’, ‘without any sensing assistance’ and ‘with ideal sensing assistance’.

Huawei, HiSilicon | Ok to further discuss | Ok to further discuss | In general, regarding sensing assisting comm., we should at least note that what sensing aspect can assist comm should be at least be studied, assuming the comm can be assisted by sensing moving objects is not the right way to go at the beginning of the study since it has been commonly acknowledged that what can assist comm is the stable and dominant paths, which clearly are not the paths caused by the moving objects. Apple |  |  | Limit to maximum Scope 2.
PCL | Yes | Yes | We support starting with SAC Scope 2a (evaluate communication performance gain with ideal sensing assistance) as a practical and focused baseline. This allows the group to first quantify the upper bound of potential benefit that sensing can bring to communication, without the added complexity of realistic sensing imperfections. Once the value is demonstrated under ideal conditions, the group can consider whether and how to introduce realistic sensing assumptions (Scope 3a/3b) in future releases.
CMCC |  |  | We support SAC Scope 1 (specifically Scope 1a) as the primary baseline for the early evaluation phase, and we do not oppose SAC Scope 3 (Scope 3a/3b) as a subsequent or optional step. However, we have reservations regarding SAC Scope 0 and Scope 2. Our detailed rationale is as follows: Support for Scope 1 / 1a (Priority): As detailed in our contribution (R1-2604027), simulating closed-loop, end-to-end communication metrics (e.g., tracking a dynamic blocker and executing proactive MAC/RRC switching) in SLS is notoriously resource-intensive and computationally prohibitive at this early stage. Evaluating realistic sensing metrics (e.g., estimation accuracy of AoA/Delay of reflectors, or blockage prediction probability) serves as a highly reliable and efficient proxy for communication gains. Scope 1a provides the best balance between standard progression speed and evaluation meaningfulness. Open to Scope 3: We do not oppose Scope 3, as it reflects the ultimate closed-loop performance. However, considering the heavy simulation burden, it should be treated as a secondary priority or left up to companies' capabilities (aligning with the spirit of Scope 1a). Against Scope 0 (No dedicated evaluation): It is widely acknowledged by both academia and industry that sensing can significantly assist communication. However, simply reusing the generic "moving object tracking" evaluations (as suggested in Scope 0) completely misses the core objective of SAC. SAC requires specific channel mapping mechanisms (e.g., tracking dynamic blockers or static dominant reflectors) that are entirely different from traditional UAV/vehicle tracking. Therefore, a dedicated evaluation scope for SAC is indispensable. Against Scope 2 (Ideal sensing assistance): Evaluating communication with "ideal sensing" brings little value to RAN1 physical-layer standardization. The fundamental purpose of the SAC evaluation is to answer the question: "How much sensing accuracy translates to how much communication gain?" We need to evaluate the sensitivity of communication performance to realistic sensing errors (e.g., how AoA/Delay estimation fluctuations impact proactive beam switching). Scope 2 completely ignores this trade-off, which defeats the purpose of designing robust ISAC reference signals.
ETRI |  |  | Scope 1 is preferred.
Sony | Comments | Comments | We are ok with Scope 0 or 1.
LGE | Comment | Comment | We prefer SAC Scope 0 at this stage. Considering the limited time for Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation, RAN1 should first focus on object detection and/or tracking. Since the 6G communication system and relevant assistance procedures are still under discussion, SAC performance evaluation seems premature for Rel-20 SI. If needed, SAC Scope 1A could be considered, where sensing measurements/results are evaluated while communication performance or performance gain is left up to companies.
NIST | Comment | Comment | To study sensing-assisted communication, both sensing and communication performance need to be studied. The sensing aspect of the evaluation should prioritize estimating Multipath Components (MPCs) to generate and maintain an RF channel knowledge map of the environment.
Nokia |  |  | We prefer to discuss SAC scope 3.
Spreadtrum | Comment | Comment | Before down-scoping, we suggest to firstly recognize and confirm the content of each scope.

Given the limited time of R20 6GR ISAC, Scope 1 can be a good attempt when considering potential assistance from sensing.

The channel similarity can consider: Similarity of the detected path, e.g., RMSE Similarity of the channel matrix H, e.g., RMSE, SGCS.

FL: Based on the input, SAC Scope 1a seems to be a possible compromise direction for the time being. The following proposal is made for Tuesday offline discussion.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, at least the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Evaluate the sensing performance where the sensing measurement/result may be able to assist communication.

Scenario: Urban Grid with EO type-2

FFS detailed evaluation assumptions, metric, etc.

Up to company to evaluate and report communication performance assisted by sensing.

Company should at least use realistic sensing measurement/result to assist communication.

Company should use the same scenario and ISAC channel as sensing performance evaluation.

Up to company to report communication-related metric and the performance gain.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-2

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, further discuss the following potential candidate approaches to assist communication:

Approach 1: Utilize sensing measurement/result of environment (e.g. wall/building, static vehicles, moving vehicles, etc.) to assist communication (e.g. beam management, CSI acquisition/prediction, etc.) of UE(s) in/near the environment.

E.g., Sense and reconstruct the wall/building, utilize reconstructed wall/building to obtain hypothesized wireless channel, to assist the beam management, CSI acquisition/prediction… of UEs.

Approach 2: Utilize sensing measurement/result of sensing target(s)/object(s) that carrying UE(s), to assist the communication performance of the UE(s).

E.g., Sense the trajectory of a human that carrying a UE, to assist the beam management, mobility… of this UE.

Approach 3: Utilize sensing resource/RS to perform communication-related measurement, and then assist the communication performance of UE(s).

E.g., Perform CSI/beam-related measurement on sensing resource/RS, to assist CSI acquisition and/or beam management.

[TABLE]

vivo | Yes | Approach 1 and Approach 2 may have not enough difference between each other. The sensing measurement/result of Approach 1 and Approach 2 is impacted by both the environment and the sensing target(s)/object(s) that carrying UE(s). Therefore, Approach is the baseline for discussion and Approach 2 can provide some additional sensing measurements, e.g., the trajectory of UE. Approach 3 is on another page (it is communication-related measurement, not sensing related measurement of Approach 1 and 2) and can be discussed separately. CMCC |  | Regarding the proposed approaches, we believe that Approach 1 and Approach 2 are intrinsically intertwined and inseparable, particularly for TRP-UE and UE-TRP sensing modes. Furthermore, we suggest adopting a use-case-driven methodology rather than prematurely debating abstract categories. Our detailed views are as follows: The Inseparability of Approach 1 and Approach 2: Effective communication assistance (SAC) inherently relies on the spatial-temporal correlation between the UE and its propagation environment. Knowing only the environment (Approach 1) is meaningless without knowing the target UE's trajectory (Approach 2), and vice versa. For example, as detailed in our contribution, for CSI Prediction, the network must simultaneously track the location/trajectory of the UE (Approach 2) and the geometry of the dominant reflectors (Approach 1) to mathematically predict the channel clusters. Therefore, treating them as mutually exclusive or separate candidate approaches is physically disconnected from reality. Prioritize Concrete Use Cases over Abstract Approaches: In the early stage of 6G ISAC evaluation, RAN1 should focus on specific, concrete SAC use cases that already have relatively mature evaluation methodologies in traditional communication studies. Currently, the industry consensus primarily revolves around concrete procedures such as Beam Management (e.g., proactive blockage avoidance) and CSI Acquisition/Prediction (e.g., pilot overhead reduction). Suggested Way Forward: We suggest that RAN1 should first down-select and agree on the concrete use cases (we highly recommend Beam Management and CSI Prediction as the starting points). Once the specific use cases are agreed upon, the required sensing approaches (whether tracking a blocker, a reflector, or the UE itself) will naturally emerge and be defined. Abstract categorization of "Approaches" should be deferred and summarized later when a comprehensive set of feasible use cases is fully stabilized.
Sony | No | The scope is still unclear at this stage. We can come back on this later
LGE |  | We prefer Approach 1. However, the SAC evaluation scope should be clarified first before discussing detailed approaches.
NIST | Yes in general | There are some overlaps between moving environmental objects and moving sensing targets.
Nokia | No | Approach 1 is okay. Approach 2 is not clear on how to associate a target with a UE. This issue should be addressed first before any study. Approach 3 is out of the scope of the 6G sensing study.
Spreadtrum | No | We suggest to discuss the SAC evaluation scopes firstly.
CATT/CICTCI | Yes | Agree to further study.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-2a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, further discuss the following potential candidate approaches to assist communication:

Approach 1: Utilize sensing measurement/result to assist the communication performance.

Sensing measurement/result may include, e.g. delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the channel, information of reconstructed wall/building, etc.

Approach 2: Utilize sensing resource/RS to perform communication-related measurement, and then assist the communication performance.

E.g., Perform CSI/beam-related measurement on sensing resource/RS, to assist CSI acquisition and/or beam management.

Others

The following views are proposed by companies.

Other potential new use cases to evaluate

Motion/gesture monitoring: NEC, Google

Environment sensing (e.g. rain, flood, fire, smoke): Qualcomm, T-Mobile USA,

Human health: NEC,

Autonomous driving: IIT Kanpur

[TABLE]
FL | Considering the limited TU, it is strongly recommended focusing on (1) object detection and/or tracking and (2) communication assistance.

Evaluation assumption

Evaluation methodology

The following evaluation methodology was agreed for Rel-19 5G-A ISAC evaluation and captured in TR 38.765 [5].

[TABLE]
6.1 Evaluation methodologies The following general procedure for performance evaluation of NR ISAC is provided. 1) Simulation parameter configuration 2) Sensing scenario generation, including the deployment of sensing Tx/Rx (STXs/SRXs) 3) Dropping N target(s), where N is equal to 0 or larger than 0 4) Channel generation and STX/SRX determination for the targets 5) Sensing signal generation and passing the sensing signal to the generated channel 6) Sensing signal processing at each SRX, optionally, sensing signal processing based on fusion from multiple STXs/SRXs - E.g., optionally, sensing signal processing based on tracking 7) Sensing performance metric calculation.

Evaluation methodology for object detection and/or tracking

Some companies (e.g. Qualcomm, DOCOMO) think the existing methodology can serve as baseline. InterDigital proposes a common methodology for 6G sensing regarding many aspects of evaluation assumption, which have similar guidance but more detailed steps.

Vivo proposes new methodology of dropping UEs and sensing targets, considering range constraint between target and UE when UE is involved in sensing.

[TABLE]
FL | In FL’s understanding, the evaluation methodology seems general enough to support both object detection and tracking. For example, it already mentions ‘sensing signal processing based on tracking’. Note that with this evaluation methodology in Rel-20 NR ISAC: Though most companies evaluated object detection, some companies already simulated tracking (e.g. ZTE (R1-2600973), CMCC (R1-2600381)). Many companies already adopted sensing data fusion from different TRPs, without explicitly mentioning this in the evaluation methodology. Based on the discussion in RAN1#124bis, most companies think that evaluation methodology in 5G-A ISAC is sufficient for object detection and/or tracking for Rel-20 6G ISAC [6]. Thus this issue will be opened only if significant change is identified.

Evaluation methodology for communication assistance

(TBD. Depend on the progress. See section 4.2)

Evaluation assumptions of baseline cases/combinations

Based on Rel-19 channel model study, Rel-20 NR ISAC evaluation, Rel-20 6G communication section, and companies’ input to this agenda in this meeting, the following evaluation assumptions for baseline cases/combinations are generated.

UAV

The following tentative table is generated for sensing UAV:

[TABLE]

System configuration | Information on sensing RS, assumed TDD UL/DL configuration if applicable | Companies to report the necessary information of sensing RS, e.g. pattern in time and frequency domain, assumption in spatial domain, sequence, TDD configuration (if applicable), etc. | Companies to report the necessary information of sensing RS, e.g. pattern in time and frequency domain, assumption in spatial domain, sequence, TDD configuration (if applicable), etc.

Simulation assumption | Overhead of sensing resource | Both Overhead 1 and Overhead 2 ((defined in TR 38.765) shall be reported Overhead 1 (Type_1 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 (Type_1 – Type_2 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 less than 10% is encouraged | Both Overhead 1 and Overhead 2 ((defined in TR 38.765) shall be reported Overhead 1 (Type_1 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 (Type_1 – Type_2 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 less than 10% is encouraged
Simulation assumption | Selection of sensing Tx-Rx pair | Option 1: a same target is modelled in the channel of single Sensing Tx/Rx Option 2: a same target is modelled in the channel of multiple Sensing Txs/Rxs Option 3: a same target is modelled in the channel of all Sensing Txs/Rxs Selection of STx-SRx pair shall be based on achievable measurement information (e.g. RSRP, pathloss, RSRPP, etc.), but not genie-aided information (e.g. location of the target, range between target and STx/SRx, etc.). | Option 1: a same target is modelled in the channel of single Sensing Tx/Rx Option 2: a same target is modelled in the channel of multiple Sensing Txs/Rxs Option 3: a same target is modelled in the channel of all Sensing Txs/Rxs Selection of STx-SRx pair shall be based on achievable measurement information (e.g. RSRP, pathloss, RSRPP, etc.), but not genie-aided information (e.g. location of the target, range between target and STx/SRx, etc.).
Simulation assumption | Tx/Rx beam information, (mt x nt) / (mr x nr) | Companies report the Tx and Rx beam information, of BS and/or UE, when applicable. | Companies report the Tx and Rx beam information, of BS and/or UE, when applicable.
Simulation assumption | Algorithm for detection | Companies report the algorithm for detection, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used | Companies report the algorithm for detection, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used
Simulation assumption | Algorithm for tracking | Companies report the algorithm for tracking, if applicable, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used | Companies report the algorithm for tracking, if applicable, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used
Simulation assumption | Other additional features | Optional Up to companies report (e.g. low power clutter, clutter mobility). | Optional Up to companies report (e.g. low power clutter, clutter mobility).

[FL1][M] Proposal 5.2-1

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, adopt the table above as the evaluation assumption for baseline case/combination of sensing UAV.

[TABLE]
Company | Yes or No | Comments
Tiami | Yes | 
IDCC | Comment | We support this proposal in principle. For baseline UAV dropping in vertical plane, we prefer “uniform distribution between 1.5 m to 300 m”. Dropping UAV targets below 25 m allow us to evaluate UAV sensing in low altitude. This is well representative of emerging low altitude use cases (e.g., drone delivery).
vivo | Yes | 
OPPO | Yes | 
Cohere | Yes | 
ZTE | Comment | Based on the proposal 4.1-1, TRP mono-static is assumed as one of baseline cases. Therefore, at least BS antenna configuration and BS transmitting power should at least consider the effect of TRP mono-static sensing mode. Otherwise, such evaluation assumption would provide too much limited information. Maybe it would be better if we finish discussing on revision for mono-static sensing mode and come back then for the evaluation assumption for baseline cases of UAV. What’s more, UE configurations are not needed for UAV use case if UE related sensing mode is not baseline.
Qualcomm | Comment | On the BS antenna configuration for 4 GHz UMa, we think we should use the same as in 5G-A ISAC study, which is the one shown in yellow now in the table, but we would like to keep it. (M, N, P, Mg, Ng; Mp, Np) for 4GHz, 4.9GHz - Configuration A - Tx: (8,8,2,1,1;4,8) - Rx: (8,8,2,1,1;4,8)
Xiaomi | [Yes] | For UMa-AV scenario: BS transmit power (per 20 MHz): The BS transmit power is suitable for non TRP-mono cases, the parameter name can be updated with “BS transmit power (per 20 MHz) for non TRP mono-static”. Besides, 49 dBm per 20MHz is preferred to achieve maximum power of 56dBm for 4GHz with 100MHz. As for the note, the descriptions for indoor can be removed since UMa-AV is outdoor only. BS antenna configuration: So many candidate options (2 or 3 for one candidate frequency) are not suitable for results comparison. One candidate value can used as baseline, others are optional. “Outdoor Combination 1” for 4GHz, “Outdoor Combination 1” for around 7Ghz, and “Outdoor Combination 1” for 30GHz can be used as baseline. As for the note, it could be revised after discussion of Proposal 5.3-1 UE antenna configuration: For Handheld UE, config 1 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz For CPE UE, config 0 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz. The Tx antenna configuration of UE should also be defined. UE antenna radiation pattern: For Handheld UE, Table 7.3-2 in TR 38.901 is assumed for antenna config with Alt 2 and isotropic radiation power pattern is assumed for Alt1, as agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda. For CPE UE, three candidate radiation pattern is agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda, maybe one can be selected as baseline. UE array orientation: For handheld UE, random in horizontal domain could be used. UE distribution: One candidate value of N could be selected as baseline. For example, N = 30 for handheld UE, and N = 30 for CPE UE. UE noise figure: One candidate value of noise figure could be selected as baseline. For example, 9dB for around 7GHz and below and 10dB for around 15GHz and above, which is usually used in NR communication evaluation. Physical information and RCS: UAV with small size could be used as baseline. Additional parameters: The minimum BS-UE distance and minimum UE-UE distance could also be defined. The minimum BS-UE 2D distance is 35m and minimum UE-UE 2D distance is 1m
NEC | Yes | 
DCM | Yes | 
Huawei, HiSilicon | Comment | Thanks to FL for the efforts to compact all the assumptions together. A few comments for further consideration/discussion: Regarding BS antenna config for UAV, we prefer to keep the same config as agreed/simulated in 5GA. Otherwise, companies need to re-run the simulations just because of the BS configuration change. Regarding UE antenna config for 7GHz, do we really need to set baseline and optional. For bandwidth, we can consider 400MHz since it is supported for comm as well.

[FL1][M] Proposal 5.2-2

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, adopt the table above as the evaluation assumption for baseline case/combination of sensing human.

[TABLE]
Company | Yes or No | Comments
Tiami | Yes | 
IDCC | Yes | 
vivo | No | Regarding the dropping of sensing targets, we propose that the supported optional number of targets includes the following values: N = 1, 2, 10, 15. Furthermore, for multi‑UE cooperative sensing, multiple UEs can be dropped to cover the entire topology. For single‑UE sensing, i.e., when sensing results from multiple UEs are not fused, the UE should not be required to sense all the targets across the entire topology or targets too far from that UE. Therefore, at least for single‑UE sensing scenarios, we propose introducing a distance constraint between the target and the UE when dropping targets; i.e., only targets within a certain distance range are required to be sensed. Regarding the UE antenna configuration, we propose to add the configuration with (M, N, P, Mg, Ng, Mp, Np) = (1, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1, 2) for dual polarization. In addition, we suggest that the number of UEs should include more values, e.g., N = 1, 10, 15, 20, 30, 60, 120, or be left up to company report.
OPPO | Yes | 
Qualcomm | Comment | We also supportive to introduce N=1
Xiaomi | [Yes] | For UMa scenario: BS transmit power (per 20 MHz): The BS transmit power is suitable for non TRP-mono cases, the parameter name can be updated with “BS transmit power (per 20 MHz) for non TRP mono-static”. Besides, 49 dBm per 20MHz is preferred to achieve maximum power of 56dBm for 4GHz with 100MHz. As for the note, the descriptions for indoor can be removed since UMa is outdoor only. BS antenna configuration: Since the table is assumptions for baseline, so many options (2 or 3 for one candidate frequency) are not suitable for results comparison. One candidate value can used as baseline, others are optional. “Outdoor Combination 1” for 4GHz, “Outdoor Combination 1” for around 7Ghz, and “Outdoor Combination 1” for 30GHz can be used as baseline. As for the note, it could be revised after discussion of Proposal 5.3-1 UE antenna configuration: For Handheld UE, config 1 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz For CPE UE, config 0 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz. The Tx antenna configuration of UE should also be defined. UE antenna radiation pattern: For Handheld UE, Table 7.3-2 in TR 38.901 is assumed for antenna config with Alt 2 and isotropic radiation power pattern is assumed for Alt1, as agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda. For CPE UE, three candidate radiation pattern is agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda, maybe one can be selected as baseline. UE array orientation: For handheld UE, random in horizontal domain could be used. UE distribution: One candidate value of N could be selected as baseline. For example, N = 30 for handheld UE, and N = 30 for CPE UE. UE noise figure: One candidate value of noise figure could be selected as baseline. For example, 9dB for around 7GHz and below and 10dB for around 15GHz and above, which is usually used in NR communication evaluation. Mobility: Option 1: 3km/h could be selected as the baseline. Minimum 3D distance between STx/SRx and sensing target: To cover all the sensing modes, it could be updated as: The minimum 2D distances between TRP and the sensing target is 35 m. The minimum 2D distances between UE and the sensing target is 1 m. Minimum target-target 3D distance: Option 2 can be used as baseline. Physical information and RCS: Option 2 for RCS model could be used as baseline. Additional parameters: The minimum BS-UE distance and minimum UE-UE distance could also be defined. The minimum BS-UE 2D distance is 35m and minimum UE-UE 2D distance is 1m. For Indoor hotspot scenario: Scenario: The Indoor - Open office could be used as baseline. BS transmit power (per 20 MHz): For the note, the descriptions for outdoor can be removed since InH is indoor. BS antenna configuration: So many candidate options (2 for one candidate frequency) are not suitable for results comparison. One candidate value can used as baseline, others are optional. “Indoor Combination 1” for 4GHz, “Indoor Combination 1” for around 7Ghz, and “Indoor Combination 1” for 30GHz can be used as baseline. As for the note, it could be revised after discussion of Proposal 5.3-1 UE antenna height: 1.5m could be used for handheld UE UE antenna configuration: For Handheld UE, config 1 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz For CPE UE, config 0 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz. The Tx antenna configuration of UE should also be defined. UE antenna radiation pattern: For Handheld UE, Table 7.3-2 in TR 38.901 is assumed for antenna config with Alt 2 and isotropic radiation power pattern is assumed for Alt1, as agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda. For CPE UE, three candidate radiation pattern is agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda, maybe one can be selected as baseline. UE array orientation: For handheld UE, random in horizontal domain could be used. UE distribution: One candidate value of N could be selected as baseline. The minimum 2D distances between UE and the sensing target is 1 m. Minimum target-target 3D distance: Option 2 can be used as baseline. The minimum BS-UE 2D distance is 0m and minimum UE-UE 2D distance is 1m.

[FL1][M] Proposal 5.2-3

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, adopt the table above as the evaluation assumption for baseline case/combination of sensing vehicle.

[TABLE]
Company | Yes or No | Comments
Tiami | Yes | 
IDCC | Yes | 
vivo | Yes | 
Xiaomi | [Yes] | For Urban grid scenario: BS transmit power (per 20 MHz): The BS transmit power is suitable for non TRP-mono cases, the parameter name can be updated with “BS transmit power (per 20 MHz) for non TRP mono-static”. Besides, 49 dBm per 20MHz is preferred to achieve maximum power of 56dBm for 4GHz with 100MHz. As for the note, the descriptions for indoor can be removed since UMa is outdoor only. BS antenna configuration: Since the table is assumptions for baseline, so many options (2 or 3 for one candidate frequency) are not suitable for results comparison. One candidate value can used as baseline, others are optional. “Outdoor Combination 1” for 4GHz, “Outdoor Combination 1” for around 7Ghz, and “Outdoor Combination 1” for 30GHz can be used as baseline. As for the note, it could be revised after discussion of Proposal 5.3-1 UE antenna height: It should be clarified the definition of Fixed UE at Roadside, not sure whether it is the RSU-type UE or CPE. UE antenna configuration: For Handheld UE, config 1 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz For fixed UE at Roadside, CPE and RSU-type UE could be discussed separately. Configuration for CPE could reuse the discussion for UAV and human. For RSU-type UE, for around 7GHz and below, 8T8R as baseline, e.g. (M, N, P, Mg, Ng; Mp, Np) = (1, 4, 2, 1, 1; 1, 4), (dH,dV) = (0.5, 0.8) λ; For 30GHz, 32T32R as baseline, e.g. (M, N, P, Mg, Ng; Mp, Np) = (1, 4, 2, 1, 4; 1, 1), (dH,dV) = (0.5, 0.5) λ. The Tx antenna configuration of UE should also be defined. UE antenna radiation pattern: For Handheld UE, Table 7.3-2 in TR 38.901 is assumed for antenna config with Alt 2 and isotropic radiation power pattern is assumed for Alt1, as agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda. For CPE UE, three candidate radiation pattern is agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda, maybe one can be selected as baseline. For vehicle, two radiation patterns are modeled in TR 37.885, option 1 could be used as baseline. UE array orientation: For handheld UE, random in horizontal domain could be used. For vehicle, follow the model for vehicle in TR 37.885 UE distribution: One candidate option could be selected as baseline. For example, option 2 for handheld UE, and option 2 for vehicle UE. For the UE type RSU (fixed UE at road side), option 2 could be used as baseline. UE noise figure: One candidate value of noise figure could be selected as baseline. For example, 9dB for around 7GHz and below and 10dB for around 15GHz and above, which is usually used in NR communication evaluation. 3D distribution: Dropping option A for Urban Grid in 37.885 could be used. Minimum target-target 3D distance: Min vehicle UE to vehicle UE distance in TR 37.775 could be uses, i.e., minimum 2m between the rear bumper of a vehicle and the front bumper of the following vehicle in the same lane.
NEC | Yes |

Simulation assumption | Selection of sensing Tx-Rx pair | Option 1: a same target is modelled in the channel of single Sensing Tx/Rx Option 2: a same target is modelled in the channel of multiple Sensing Txs/Rxs Option 3: a same target is modelled in the channel of all Sensing Txs/Rxs Selection of STx-SRx pair shall be based on achievable measurement information (e.g. RSRP, pathloss, RSRPP, etc.), but not genie-aided information (e.g. location of the target, range between target and STx/SRx, etc.).
Simulation assumption | Tx/Rx beam information, (mt x nt) / (mr x nr) | Companies report the Tx and Rx beam information, of BS and/or UE, when applicable.
Simulation assumption | Algorithm for detection | Companies report the algorithm for detection, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used
Simulation assumption | Algorithm for tracking | Companies report the algorithm for tracking, if applicable, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used
Simulation assumption | Other additional features | Optional Up to companies report (e.g. low power clutter, clutter mobility).

Companies’ input on sensing mode specific discussion is collected as follows:

Simultaneous v.s. non-simultaneous transmission of TRP mono-static

Only half of the AE and TxRU are used when operating simultaneously: ZTE, Qualcomm

Note: no switching time for this case.

Full AE and TxRU are used when operating non-simultaneously, with switching time: Qualcomm, Lenovo

Switching time:

Case 0: 0 us: Lenovo

Case 1: 0.5 us: Qualcomm

Case 2: 3 us: Qualcomm

Case 3: 7 us: Lenovo

Case 4: 10 us: Lenovo

Only simultaneous: ZTE, Spreadtrum, Tiami

FL: Based on companies input, the following proposal is made. Note that, referring to RAN4 requirement is fine. Moreover, RAN4 only set ‘minimum requirement’. The actual capability is usually better than RAN4 requirement. By the way, 0 us seems too optimistic. As middle ground, we may consider 1 us and 5 us, representing optimistic value and conservative value respectively.

Qualcomm propose the following error modelling for time error:

[TABLE]
UE-UE bistatic: Timing Error between two UEs connected to the same TRP Truncated Gaussian distribution with σ = 10 ns TRP-TRP, TRP-UE, UE-TRP bistatic: Timing Error between two TRPs: Truncated Gaussian distribution with σ = 1 ns (tightly synchronized) σ = 5 ns (conservative scenario)

[TABLE]
Parameter | Proposed values | Distribution | Notes

[TABLE]
TRP-TRP With timing error, modelled by a truncated Gaussian distribution within ±10 ns for ≤ 7 GHz and ±3 ns for 30 GHz UE-UE Up to companies report TRP-UE Option 2: With error, truncated Gaussian model with σ = 30 ns for timing error UE-TRP Option 2: FFS

Frequency error/offset: vivo (optional), Qualcomm, DOCOMO, ETRI, Tiami

Qualcomm proposes the following error modelling for frequency error:

[TABLE]
TRP-UE, UE-TRP bistatic: Frequency Error at a UE relative to TRP: Before Initial acquisition: Uniform distribution ±5 / ±10 / ±20 ppm After initial acquisition, before fine T/F tracking: Uniform distribution ±0.1 ppm After fine T/F tracking: Uniform distribution ±0.01 ppm Drift rate at a UE: 0.2 ppm/sec Drift rate at a TRP: TBD ppm/sec Frequency Error at a TRP : Uniform distribution ±0.05 ppm

[TABLE]
Parameter | Proposed values | Distribution | Notes
Frequency error (CFO) | {10, 50} ppb | Per oscillator pair | 10 ppb = high-quality TCXO; 50 ppb = typical UE

ETRI proposes the following error modelling for frequency error:

[TABLE]
TRP-TRP With frequency error (e.g., carrier frequency offset), modelled by uniform distribution +/-0.05 ppm (TR 38.857) UE-UE With frequency error (e.g., carrier frequency offset), modelled by uniform distributions +/-0.1 ppm(UE) TRP-UE Option 2: With error, σ = 0.05 ppm for frequency error Option 3: With frequency error (e.g., carrier frequency offset), modelled by uniform distributions +/-0.05 ppm(TRP) and +/-0.1 ppm(UE) UE-TRP Option 3: With frequency error (e.g., carrier frequency offset), modelled by uniform distributions +/-0.05 ppm(TRP) and +/-0.1 ppm(UE)

Tiami proposes 0.1 ppm for TRP-TRP bi-static and TRP-UE bi-static

FL: Based on companies input, the following proposal is made. Note that, ideal case is strongly preferred by majority, which is reasonable to have a practical, simple start. Moreover, it is indeed several methods to calibrate the error by implementation. It is preferred not to make non-ideal case too complicated at the beginning.

Coherency and phase continuity among symbols within a CPI

Always coherent only/as baseline: vivo, ZTE, CATT/CICTCI, DOCOMO, LGE, Spreadtrum, Sharp, China Telecom

Conditionally breaking coherency: vivo (optional), Qualcomm, Apple, Interdigital, ETRI, Tiami

DL/UL switching: vivo (optional), ETRI, Tiami

Timing drift and TA: vivo (optional)

N (FFS value of N) Breaking events randomly during CPI: Qualcomm (due to multiple reasons)

Qualcomm identifies that coherency can be maintained only if the following conditions are met:

[TABLE]
Category | Condition
RF chains | TX/RX chains remain continuously powered and synchronized
Antennas | No antenna switching within the CPI
Power & calibration | No power adaption, gain change, or RF recalibration during the CPI
Scheduling | No DL/UL switching or TDD direction change inside the CPI
Symbol type | Same symbol type (e.g., DL, UL, SBFD/non-SBFD) throughout the CPI
Resource allocation | Same frequency-domain allocation (RBs/BWP) across the CPI
Other factors | No BWP change, no RRC state change

Limited by coherent time: Apple, ETRI

Apple: T_coh = {1, 5, 10} ms for UE-involved bistatic (TCXO); T_coh = {10, 20, 50} ms for TRP-TRP bistatic (OCXO). Beyond T_coh, non-coherent integration is assumed.

FL: Based on the input, the following proposal is made. ‘Always coherent’ is strongly preferred by majority. At the same time, many conditions and modelling methods are proposed. Among all, DL/UL switching is well supported. But other conditions are also reasonable based on Rel-17 Coverage Enhancement Study, on joint channel estimation. It is preferred to take some typical or simple conditions at the beginning, not to make non-ideal case too complicated.

Qualcomm proposes the following table, to model the error as truncated Gaussian models for all errors with standard deviation σ and truncated between [-2σ, 2σ]:

[TABLE]
UE type | Location error (2D) | Z-axis Error | Velocity error | Orientation error (yaw, roll, pitch)
CPE | σ = [0.05-0.1] m | σ = [0.05-0.1] m | Perfectly known (static) | σ = [0.1°]
Handheld UE, outdoor | σ = [0.5 – 2] m | σ = [3–6] m | σ = [0.05–0.2] m/s | σ = [2–5°]
Handheld UE, indoor | σ = [0.5 – 3] m | σ = [1–2] m | σ = [0.1–0.5] m/s | σ = [2–5°]
Vehicular UE | σ = [0.1–0.5] m | σ = [0.1–0.5] m | σ = [0.02–0.1] m/s | σ = [0.1°]

velocity error σ = {1, 3} m/s per axis

orientation error σ = {5°, 15°} per axis

Metric and performance target

Metric

The following sensing-related requirements are agreed in RAN plenary and captured in TR 38.914. Note that such 3GPP RAN internal requirements are completely aligned with ITU-R requirements.

[TABLE]
5.1.17 Sensing-related requirements Sensing-related capabilities are measured in terms of the following technical performance requirements: • Detection Probability and False Alarm Probability: Detection probability is the probability of correctly detecting the presence of the sensing target when it is present, and false alarm probability is the associated probability that a sensing target is detected when no corresponding true sensing target is present. • Horizontal/Vertical Localization Accuracy: It is defined as the absolute difference between the estimated horizontal/vertical location and the actual horizontal/vertical location of the sensing target. The value of localization accuracy shall be obtained assuming 90% confidence level, which is the 90th percentile point of the CDF of the location estimation error. Both the horizontal and vertical localization accuracy are estimated based on Cartesian coordinates. • Velocity Accuracy: It is defined as the absolute difference between the estimated velocity and the actual velocity of the sensing target. The value of velocity accuracy shall be obtained assuming 90% confidence level, which is the 90th percentile point of the CDF of the velocity estimation error. In the above technical performance requirements, the sensing target is a passive object, which is not connected to the radio network for communication.

In 5G NR, RAN1 concluded the following performance metrics for sensing, which are nicely captured in TR 38.765 [5]:

[TABLE]
4.1 Definitions of performance metrics In the evaluation of NR ISAC, the following performance metrics are considered: - Horizontal/vertical positioning accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated horizontal/vertical position and the corresponding true position of a sensing target. NOTE: There should be only one estimated horizontal/vertical position corresponding to the true position of a sensing target. - Velocity accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated velocity and the corresponding true velocity of a sensing target. For single TRP monostatic sensing, both the radial velocity accuracy and the 3D velocity accuracy can be estimated. The true radial velocity is the projection of true 3D velocity on the direction from TRP to target for TRP monostatic. NOTE: There should be only one estimated velocity corresponding to the true velocity of a sensing target. - Missed detection probability is defined as the conditional probability of not detecting the presence of a target when the target is actually present in the simulation area. where, - is the number of missed targets in the drop n, i.e., the true target not associated with any detected object. - is the number of true targets in the drop n. - is total number of drops with at least one target per drop - False alarm probability Type 1 is defined for cases without true target dropped in simulation area. An object detected when there is no target present in the simulation area is considered a false alarm. where, - is equal to 1 if at least one object is detected when there is no target dropped in the simulation area in the drop n, otherwise is equal to 0. - is the total number of drops without target in the simulation area. - False alarm probability Type 2 is defined for cases with true target dropped in simulation area. An object detected but not associated with any true target in the simulation area is considered as a false alarm. where, - is the number of detected objects but not associated with any true targets in the drop n. - is the total number of detected objects in the drop n. - is number of drops with at least one detected object. - is the total number of drops with target(s) in the simulation area. NOTE: Both False alarm probability types are mandatory.

All the metrics are about object detection and/or tracking. It can be observed that sensing metrics in 5G are aligned with those in ITU-R and RAN Plenary, with some refinement on two types of false alarm probability. Therefore, in FL’s understanding, these metrics are suitable for the evaluation in 6G, in the use cases of object detection and/or tracking. No need to argue whether these metrics are applicable or not.

DOCOMO raised an issue that the False alarm probability Type 2 may be problematic (‘However, the count could include detected objects with high accuracy but not associated with the corresponding true target as the true target is associated with another detected object with higher accuracy. We believe that this count is invalid as in practical, these two detected objects will be identified.’).

[TABLE]
FL | @DOCOMO, this may not be an issue, since in 5G-A we already clarify the following [5], which allows one on one mapping at most. We can open some discussion if such issue is still controversial to you.

Inclusive metric definition for object detection/tracking

Some companies (e.g. CATT/CICTCI, Huawei, Samsung, OPPO, Ericsson, Tiami, etc.) point out that revision is needed to make the metrics more inclusive. The proposals include:

For single pair of bi-static sensing, provide the explanation of ‘radial velocity’;

CATT/CICTCI, OPPO, Ericsson provide very detailed analysis and derivation, and derive that the measurable Doppler at SRx will be: , and subsequently, assuming STx and SRx are static, the formula can be deduced as .

In addition, considering the situation that STx and SRx may be a moving device, the vector projected on incident/scattering direction, is not just the ‘3D velocity vector of the target’, but should be ‘velocity difference vector between 3D velocity vector of the target and the 3D velocity vector of STx/SRx:

,

Replace ‘single TRP mono-static’ with more general description, such as ‘single node mono-static’;

Note that, if we cannot reach clear and inclusive definition of radial velocity for bi-static sensing, companies have to evaluate 3D velocity accuracy for bi-static sensing, as also proposed by Interdigital.

The following proposals are made based on the input.

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.1-1

For object detection and/or tracking, update the definition of velocity accuracy for 6G ISAC evaluation:

Velocity accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated velocity and the corresponding true velocity of a sensing target. For single node TRP monostatic sensing or single pair of bistatic sensing, both the radial velocity accuracy and the 3D velocity accuracy can be estimated. The true radial velocity is the projection of true 3D velocity on the direction from TRP to target. the sum of the following two components:

Component 1: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the direction from target to STX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the STX;

Component 2: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the direction from target to SRX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the SRX.

Note: If STx and SRx are static, true radial velocity is , where is the true 3D velocity of the target, is the bistatic angle between incident direction and scattering direction, and is the angle between the true 3D velocity vector and the bisector vector within the bistatic angle . For mono-static sensing, .
