Statistics for multi-scattering point targets

In Rel-20 NR ISAC study, the sensing target is restricted to UAV, which is modelled only as single scattering point objects. In 6G, there may be an issue that how to make statistics when the target/object is modelled with multi-scattering points, e.g. vehicle, AGV.

In general, there are two possible ways, one is to calculate metrics per target/object, and the other one is to calculate the metrics per scattering point. Quite some companies (e.g. CATT/CICTCI, xiaomi, Interdigital, China Telecom,) think ‘per target/object’ should be adopted. What’s more, some companies also propose to use single scattering point in Rel-20 evaluation, which can avoid this issue.

[TABLE]
FL | FL agrees the analysis from the companies above. Since the performance target from RAN Plenary and ITU-R does not differentiate single scattering point or multi-scattering point, statistics of the metrics should be ‘per target/object’ by natural, unless they clearly stated otherwise. If it is still controversial, we can open the discussion if necessary after other essential issues have sufficient progress.

Existing metrics for tracking

For tracking, while the accuracy of localization and velocity estimation can reuse those for detection (i.e. per point per time stamp), the missed detection or false alarm probability may be defined in trajectory level (CATT/CICTCI) or per target/object level in individual time (xiaomi, Interdigital, China Telecom,).

[TABLE]
FL | Based on the discussion in RAN1#124bis [6], some companies mentioned that current statistics is also applicable to both detection and tracking (per point per CPI/time stamp). Reusing the same statistic granularity can also provide visible difference between object detection and tracking. Unless serious issue is identified, FL think we can assume legacy statistics method (per point per CPI/time instance) still applies to tracking.

Other metrics for object detection and/or tracking

Other than the existing performance metrics, some companies propose to introduce new metrics to reflect the quality of trajectory from tracking. For example:

Sensing latency: Nokia, Qualcomm, T-Mobile USA, Lenovo, Google,

Not needed since latency is E2E requirement: xiaomi,

Sensing refreshing rate: Nokia, Lenovo, T-Mobile USA, InterDigital,

Already reflected by overhead: xiaomi,

Sensing resolution: Nokia, T-Mobile USA, Google

Sensing overhead/CPI: Google

Sensing capacity: Google

Sensing range accuracy: Apple

Classification accuracy and counting resolution: NTHU

Tracking specific metric (e.g., consistency, integrity): CATT/CICTCI

Signal related metric (e.g. PAPR, AF, RF emission, correlation, etc.): Qualcomm

Link level/signal level metrics (CP, power, etc.): NEC

System level communication metrics (throughput, BLER, etc.): Tejas, NEC,

Integration/harmonization between sensing & communication: Qualcomm, NEC

Joint Mutual Information (JMI): PCL

[TABLE]
FL | Recall that, there is no clear performance requirement for any potential new metrics from RAN Plenary or ITU-R so far. Some metrics have been discussed in Rel-20 NR ISAC but not agreeable. Some metrics are from SA1 but usually SA1’s requirement is from view of service rather than RAN1 simulation. Also, some of them seem to be ‘intermediate metrics’, not directly reflecting the performance of object detection and/or tracking, but provide some insight of sensing indirectly. In RAN1#124bis [6] and also this meeting, there is no enough support for any of the metric listed above. Given the situation and limited time, it is suggested focusing on essential issues and existing metrics first. Discussion on additional metrics may be opened only if we have sufficient progress.

Performance target

The following sensing-related requirements are agreed in RAN plenary and captured in TR 38.914 [2]. Note that such 3GPP RAN internal requirement is completely aligned with ITU-R requirement.

[TABLE]
5.1.17 Sensing-related requirements Sensing-related capabilities are measured in terms of the following technical performance requirements: • Detection Probability and False Alarm Probability: Detection probability is the probability of correctly detecting the presence of the sensing target when it is present, and false alarm probability is the associated probability that a sensing target is detected when no corresponding true sensing target is present. • Horizontal/Vertical Localization Accuracy: It is defined as the absolute difference between the estimated horizontal/vertical location and the actual horizontal/vertical location of the sensing target. The value of localization accuracy shall be obtained assuming 90% confidence level, which is the 90th percentile point of the CDF of the location estimation error. Both the horizontal and vertical localization accuracy are estimated based on Cartesian coordinates. • Velocity Accuracy: It is defined as the absolute difference between the estimated velocity and the actual velocity of the sensing target. The value of velocity accuracy shall be obtained assuming 90% confidence level, which is the 90th percentile point of the CDF of the velocity estimation error. In the above technical performance requirements, the sensing target is a passive object, which is not connected to the radio network for communication. The minimum requirements for Sensing-related requirements are summarized in Table 5.1.17-1. The minimum requirements for Sensing-related requirements are summarized in Table 5.1.17-1.

The exact Table 5.1.17-1 in TR 38.914 is copied below:

Table 5.1.17-1: Sensing-related requirements

[TABLE]
 | Indoor Factory | Urban Macro
Detection Probability | 95% | 95%
False Alarm Probability | 5% | 5%
Horizontal Localization Accuracy | 2 m | 5 m
Vertical Localization Accuracy | N/A | 8 m (Note 1)
Velocity Accuracy | 2 m/s | 4 m/s

Note 1: The applicability of vertical localization accuracy depends on the type of sensing target.

Performance target for object detection and/or tracking in UMa and Indoor Factory

As highlighted in cyan, the requirements are set assuming 90% confidence level. Hence it is better to clarify this condition in RAN1 to make the target performance clear.

Detection probability = 1 - Missed Detection Probability, naturally. Hence, the required Missed Detection Probability should be 5% (derived from 1 – 95%).

There is another issue regarding false alarm probability. RAN1 defined two types of false alarm probability, i.e. Type 1 and Type 2, corresponding to ‘without target’ condition and ‘with target’ condition, respectively. But RAN Plenary and ITU-R only mention one requirement, i.e. 5%. OPPO proposes to drop Type 1 since the current definition is more closed to Type 2 in their reading. Some other companies (e.g. DOCOMO, Lenovo, Tejas, Qualcomm, InterDigital,) indicate that 5% can be assumed for both Type 1 and Type 2. From FL perspective, to be safe, we can assume that this requirement (5%) can be applied to both False Alarm Probability Type 1 and Type 2.

Note that current requirements do not differentiate sensing target/objects or frequencies. These values have been debated for a long time in ITU-R and RAN Plenary and finally converged. Some companies (e.g. Huawei, Qualcomm, NIST) propose new values for specific sensing targets, which are also discussed in some contributions but no clear suggestion (e.g. Samsung, Nokia). FL suggests no target/object specific requirements at this time, unless clearly required by RAN Plenary or ITU-R.

Speaking of this, RAN Plenary’s requirement clearly states that applicability of vertical localization accuracy depends on the type of sensing target. As mentioned by quite some companies (e.g. InterDigital, Ericsson), it is better to clarify that this is only applicable to the case of sensing UAV.

All in all, the following proposal is made based on the observations.

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.2-1

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation, for object detection and/or tracking, regarding the performance target in UMa and Indoor Factory scenarios given by RAN Plenary:

The performance objective on False Alarm Probability (5%) applies to both False Alarm Probability Type 1 and False Alarm Probability Type 2.

The vertical localization accuracy is only applicable to the case of sensing UAV.

Performance target for object detection and/or tracking in other scenarios

RAN Plenary and ITU-R only set requirements for the scenarios of Indoor Factory and UMa.

FL | No doubt that it is RAN1’s obligation to fulfil the study task from RAN Plenary and ITU-R, while RAN1’s study scope is not limited to these two scenarios. Current requirements on Indoor Factory and UMa are converged after long-time tough discussion in ITU-R. RAN Plenary directly adopted these values in RAN#111. In current requirement for UMa and Indoor Factory, all possible sensing targets, frequencies and conditions have been taken into account. It’s sceptical whether we can tune the requirements a little higher or lower on top of existing values for other scenarios, and what’s underlining message with the modified values. For other scenarios, with or without performance target does not impact RAN1 study on evaluation, including evaluation methodology, assumptions, simulation and performance statistics, as well as channel modelling. Therefore, FL suggests postponing the discussion to (1) see whether RAN Plenary will give additional guidance in Jun. 2026, (2) monitor the overall progress in other essential aspects.

Extension of channel model for sensing

Considering the very limited TU, we need to be extremely careful on the study direction and the scope of channel modeling extension.

Trajectory other than uniform linear motion

Trajectory was discussed a bit in Rel-19, but not widely studied. In Rel-19, for all sensing targets, only constant velocity is agreed. And hence, by default, the trajectories for all sensing targets are uniform linear motion derived from constant velocity, without any disturbance. We also call it ‘straight-line trajectory’ which is captured in TR 38.901 [7].

Some companies think such ‘straight-line trajectory’ is sufficient for Rel-20 study:

Straight-line trajectory: MTK, xiaomi, CATT/CICTCI, Ericsson, OPPO,

Some companies think trajectory other than straight-line trajectory (uniform linear motion derived from constant velocity, without disturbance) is needed for the study, especially for the use case of tracking. At the same time, some other companies have the opposite view. These companies show preference on new trajectory model in Rel-20:

Kinematic/maneuvering models, e.g. state-transition equation with CV + CA + additive process noise, in tangent and/or radial direction: Nokia, vivo, ZTE (noise only)

Note: companies use different but similar terms to describe such non-linear trajectory. We can discuss the suitable terms, if necessary.

In last meeting, majority companies do not think trajectory other than uniform linear motion is needed [6]. The situation is not changed in this meeting. Meanwhile, it should be noted that tracking is in Rel-20 scope. The following proposal is suggested as a compromise.

[FL1][H] Proposal 7.2-1

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation, for tracking sensing targets/objects, consider the following trajectory model:

Baseline: Straight-light trajectory, i.e. uniform linear motion derived from constant velocity without disturbance

Other trajectories can be optionally reported by companies

[TABLE]
Company | Yes or No | Comments
Tiami | Yes | We agree with the baseline.
IDCC | Yes | We support the FL proposal
vivo | No | If straight-light trajectory is the assumption, in theory, we don’t need to have any measurements for tracking. This is because tracking estimator, e.g., Kalman filter, will put 100% trusty on state equation. For example, with Kalman filter, after a while, updated K gain becomes zero due to zero noise assumption on state equation.
OPPO | Yes | 
ZTE | No | Straight-light trajectory would make the evaluation on tracking unconvincing. If it is used, as long as velocity is estimated, position of sensing target on any time point could be derived by starting position and total time step. That’s what will happen in Kalman filter as vivo said. Then the sensing algorithm would generate sensing result based on historical measurements, not based on current measurements, making sensing as “purely predicting”. We propose to use constant velocity(CV) model with velocity noise as baseline, which is already widely used in radar field.

LLS channel model

Views on LLS channel modeling were collected in RAN1#124bis [6]. On how to model LLS channel, the following methods were discussed by companies in this meeting:

Option 1: Enhanced CDL/TDL model

Option 1-1: Based on existing LLS channel model (CDL or TDL), and add one or more clusters representing target(s)

Option 1-2: Based on existing LLS channel model (CDL or TDL), and update the parameters of one or more cluster to represent target(s)

Option 2: Simplified system level evaluation

Option 2-1: Reducing the system level channel model for ISAC to single target, reduced Tx and Rx

Option 2-2: Limiting the system level simulation to non-cooperative sensing

Option 3: The Tx-target link and target-Rx link are modelled by separate CDL channels, concatenation is then performed to get the target channel; the background channel is modelled by another CDL channel.

In this meeting, companies’ input on LLS channel modeling is summarized as follows:

Support in general: CMCC, Nokia, Lenovo,

Option 1:

Option 1-1: xiaomi, ZTE, InterDigital,

Option 1-2: InterDigital, Tejas,

Option 2:

Option 2-1: ZTE, Apple, MTK, Spreadtrum, ETRI

Option 2-2: xiaomi

Option 3:

It seems Option 2-1 is slightly preferred by majority. Strictly speaking, Option 2-1 is not an LLS channel modelling, but simplified SLS. The largest advantage of Option 2-1 is that no channel model extension is needed – remaining work is to define the simplified evaluation assumptions.

Note that the discussion on LLS channel modeling will be opened only if the necessity is confirmed in Agenda 10.8.2 or Agenda 10.8.3.

[POSTPONE] Proposal 7.3-1

(Place holder for discussing the direction of LLS channel modelling/SLS simplification, depending on the conclusion on necessity of LLS/simplified SLS from AI 10.8.2 or 10.8.3)

Company | Yes or No | Comments
Tiami | Yes | We postpone the discussion and wait for 10.8.3 conclusions.

RAN1 #124bis

Working Assumption

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation,

evaluate object detection and/or tracking for the following sensing targets/objects: UAV, human, vehicle, AGV

discuss further on evaluation on communication assistance

Conclusion

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation, the attributes (e.g. antenna pattern, mobility, etc.) of different UEs are for the purpose of evaluation discussion. No intention to study or specify diverse device type.

Agreement:

For Rel-20 6G sensing performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, the following cases/combinations are considered as baseline cases/combinations.

[TABLE]
Sensing target | Indoor or outdoor | Scenario | Carrier frequency
UAV | Outdoor | UMa-AV, SMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for UMa-AV only)
Human | Outdoor | UMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz
Human | Indoor | Indoor Hotspot | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz
Vehicle | Outdoor | Urban Grid, Highway | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for Urban Grid only)
AGV | Indoor | Indoor factory (SH) | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz

Note: sensing mode(s) for each row will be discussed

Other cases/combinations can also be evaluated, reported and collected from companies.

Note: In this agreement, no intention to de-prioritize other scenarios or carrier frequencies.

Agreement:

For object detection and/or tracking, define velocity accuracy for 6G ISAC evaluation:

Velocity accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated velocity and the corresponding true velocity of a sensing target. For single node monostatic sensing or single pair of bistatic sensing, both the radial velocity accuracy and the 3D velocity accuracy can be estimated. The true radial velocity is the sum of the following two components:

Component 1: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to STX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the STX;

Component 2: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to SRX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the SRX.

Note: If STx and SRx are static, true radial velocity is , where is the true 3D velocity of the target, is the bistatic angle between incident direction and scattering direction, and is the angle between the true 3D velocity vector and the bisector vector within the bistatic angle . For mono-static sensing, .

Agreement:

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, for object detection and/or tracking, regarding the performance target in UMa and Indoor Factory scenarios given by RAN Plenary:

The vertical localization accuracy requirement is applicable to the case of sensing UAV.

## R1-2603929 - R1-2603929_Moderator (CATT)_Moderator summary #5 on ISAC evaluations.docx
Source: E:\标准相关资料\RAN1_125_ISAC_文档整理_20260616\RAN1_125_ISAC\01_Evaluations\Moderator_Summaries\R1-2603929_Moderator (CATT)_Moderator summary #5 on ISAC evaluations.docx

3GPP TSG RAN WG1 #125 R1-2603929

Dalian, China, May 18th – 22nd, 2026

Agenda item: 10.8.1

Source: Moderator (CATT)

Title: Moderator summary #5 on ISAC evaluations

Document for: Discussion/Decision

Introduction

In 6G Radio study, RAN1 is tasked to evaluate sensing performance and if necessary, extend channel modelling for the selected use cases [1]. The relevant scenarios and requirements can be found in [2]. Per RAN1 Chairman’s guidance, evaluation of sensing in this meeting is planned as follows.

[TABLE]
10.8.1 Evaluations Note 1: Including deployment scenarios, performance evaluation methodologies/metrics/KPIs for the target use cases, and potential extension of channel model, if necessary

The following email thread is assigned for this agenda:

[TABLE]
[125-R20-6GR-ISAC-Evaluations] Email discussion on Rel-20 6GR-ISAC-Evaluations–Yongqiang (CATT) To be used for sharing updates on online/offline schedule, details on what is to be discussed in online/offline sessions, tdoc number of the moderator summary for online session, etc.

This document summarizes the contributions and discussions on Agenda 10.8.1 (Evaluations) in this meeting.

Proposal marks

In this document, issues, questions and proposals are tagged and colored with the following rules.

The proposals in this document are tagged and color coded respectively

[H] Proposal: High Priority, to be handled firstly.

[M] Proposal: Medium Priority, which may be handled on best effort basis or with clear majority view.

[L] Proposal: Low Priority, which may be treated only if there is clear consensus.

In current round for discussion, issues to be discussed are further tagged with [FL5]. Note: In case there is more than one round discussion, some proposals without update are still marked with early round number. If any company hasn’t provided a comment yet, or prefer to update the comments, feel free to comment on them.

File uploading

When uploading draft files for email discussion, if needed, please follow the naming convention in this example:

ISACEvaFLS-v000-Moderator.docx

ISACEvaFLS-v001-Moderator-CompanyA.docx

ISACEvaFLS-v002-CompanyA-CompanyB.docx

ISACEvaFLS-v003-CompanyB-CompanyC.docx

If needed, you may “lock” a spreadsheet file for 30 minutes by creating a checkout file, as in this example:

Assume CompanyC wants to update ISACEvaFLS-v002-CompanyA-CompanyB.docx.

CompanyC uploads an empty file named ISACEvaFLS-v003-CompanyB-CompanyC.checkout

CompanyC checks that no one else has created a checkout file simultaneously, and if there is a collision, CompanyC tries to coordinate with the company who made the other checkout (see, e.g., contact list below).

CompanyC then has 30 minutes to upload ISACEvaFLS-v003-CompanyB-CompanyC.docx

If no update is uploaded in 30 minutes, other companies can ignore the checkout file.

Note that the file timestamps on the server are in UTC time.

Online proposals

Monday

N/A

Tuesday

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.1-1on

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, for the baseline cases/combinations, encourage companies to improve the sensing performance, e.g. by exploring different sensing modes.

Note: Evaluating any sensing mode in this agenda does not necessarily mean any RAN1 specification impact.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-1on

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, at least the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Evaluate the performance of sensing measurement/results, where the sensing measurement/result may be able to assist communication.

Scenario: Urban Grid

Sensing measurement/result to assist communication may include, e.g., delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the channel, information of reconstructed wall/building, etc. FFS details.

FFS detailed evaluation assumptions, metric, etc.

Up to company to evaluate and report communication performance assisted by sensing.

Company should at least use realistic sensing measurement/result to assist communication.

Company should use the same scenario and ISAC channel as sensing performance evaluation.

Up to company to report communication-related metric and the corresponding performance gain.

Baseline: Straight-line constant velocity trajectory

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.1-1on

For object detection and/or tracking, update the definition of velocity accuracy for 6G ISAC evaluation:

Velocity accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated velocity and the corresponding true velocity of a sensing target. For single node TRP monostatic sensing or single pair of bistatic sensing, both the radial velocity accuracy and the 3D velocity accuracy can be estimated. The true radial velocity is the projection of true 3D velocity on the direction from TRP to target. the sum of the following two components:

Component 1: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to STX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the STX;

Component 2: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to SRX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the SRX.

Note: If STx and SRx are static, true radial velocity is , where is the true 3D velocity of the target, is the bistatic angle between incident direction and scattering direction, and is the angle between the true 3D velocity vector and the bisector vector within the bistatic angle . For mono-static sensing, .

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.2-1on

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, for object detection and/or tracking, regarding the performance target in UMa and Indoor Factory scenarios given by RAN Plenary:

The performance objective on False Alarm Probability (5%) applies to both False Alarm Probability Type 1 and False Alarm Probability Type 2.

The vertical localization accuracy requirement is only applicable to the case of sensing UAV.

Option 3: Coherency is not maintained due to N event(s) that randomly distributed in a CPI length Random phase is added when there is a breaking event. Alt 1: N = 1 Alt 2: N > 1, value of N is up to company report

Note: Added random phase is up to companies report, e.g. (-pi, pi], or residual offset (-a, a] where a < pi.

[ON2][M] Proposal 5.3-6-on

[ON2][M] Proposal 6.1-1a-on

For object detection and/or tracking, update the definition of velocity accuracy for 6G ISAC evaluation:

Velocity accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated velocity and the corresponding true velocity of a sensing target. For single node monostatic sensing or single pair of bistatic sensing, both the radial velocity accuracy and the 3D velocity accuracy can be estimated. The true radial velocity is the sum of the following two components:

Component 1: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to STX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the STX;

Component 2: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to SRX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the SRX.

Note: If STx and SRx are static, true radial velocity is , where is the true 3D velocity of the target, is the bistatic angle between incident direction and scattering direction, and is the angle between the true 3D velocity vector and the bisector vector within the bistatic angle . For mono-static sensing, .

The vertical localization accuracy requirement is applicable to the case of sensing UAV.

[ON3][H] Proposal 4.2-1c

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, at least the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Further discuss the sensing measurement/result used to assist communication, which may include, e.g.:

Delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the ISAC channel

Information of detected objects (static or moving) and/or large size surface (e.g. EO type-2)

Companies are encouraged to evaluate the performance of sensing measurement/results used to assist communication.

Scenario: Urban Grid with EO type-2.

Urban Grid without EO type-2 and other scenarios are not precluded.

FFS detailed evaluation assumptions, sensing modes, prior information of EO-type2, etc.

Companies to report:

Details of performance metrics on sensing measurement/result used to assist communication

How such sensing measurement/result is able to assist the communication

The corresponding performance

Companies are encouraged to evaluate and report communication performance assisted by sensing.

SAC use cases that agreed in AI 10.8.2 are considered as starting point.

Company should clarify whether ideal/non-ideal sensing measurement/result is used to assist communication.

Company should use the same scenario and ISAC channel as sensing performance evaluation.

Company to report:

Communication-related metric

Detailed methods on how the sensing measurement/result assist the communication

The corresponding performance gain

Friday

[ON4][H] Proposal 5.2-4c

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, adopt the following evaluation assumption table for baseline case/combination of sensing AGV.

[TABLE]
 | Parameter | Sensing AGV

System configuration | Information on sensing RS, assumed TDD UL/DL configuration if applicable | Companies to report the necessary information of sensing RS, e.g. pattern in time and frequency domain, assumption in spatial domain, sequence, TDD configuration (if applicable), etc.

System configuration | Layout | ISD = 50 m Follow the layout (InF-SH, sparse clutter, high BS) in TR 38.901/TR 38.914
System configuration | Wrap-round | No wrap-round
BS configuration | BS antenna height | 8 m
BS configuration | BS transmit power (per 20 MHz) | For 4 GHz and ~7 GHz 24 dBm Note: BS Tx power scales up with bandwidth proportionally under the limitation of the maximum BS Tx power is 33 dBm for indoor for the above carrier frequencies. For 30 GHz 23 dBm Note: For around 30GHz, BS Tx power scales up with bandwidth proportionally under the limitation of EIRP 75dBm. Other BS transmit powers are not precluded (companies to report) Note: Tx power may be further limited due to mono-static sensing.

CPE: A number of N = {1, 10, 20, 30(baseline), 60, 90} UEs uniformly distributed in the horizontal area of the convex hull. Other values are not precluded.

Sensing target/object configuration (vehicle) | Outdoor/indoor | 100% Indoor
Sensing target/object configuration (vehicle) | Physical information and RCS | Size (Length x Width x Height) Option 1: 0.5 m x 1.0 m x 0.5 m Option 2: 1.5 m x 3.0m x 1.5 m RCS based on Clause 7.9.2.1 in TR 38.901 Option 1: single scattering point
Sensing target/object configuration (vehicle) | 3D distribution | N targets uniformly distributed over the horizontal area of the convex hull Baseline: N = 5 Optional: N = 1, 2, 10, 15 NOTE: N = 0 may be considered for the evaluation of false alarm
Sensing target/object configuration (vehicle) | Mobility | Option 1: Uniform distribution in the range of up to 30 km/h Option 2: Fixed velocities {3, 10} km/h
Sensing target/object configuration (vehicle) | Orientation | Random, horizontal plane only
Sensing target/object configuration (vehicle) | Trajectory | Up to company report

Sensing target/object configuration (vehicle) | Minimum target-target 3D distance | Option A: At least larger than the physical size of a target Option B: Fixed value, 5 m
Sensing target/object configuration (vehicle) | LOS/NLOS | LOS and NLOS
Simulation assumption | Inter-site interference | Option 1: Not modelled Option 2: Explicitly modelled Including interference from communication signal, if applicable.
Simulation assumption | Co-site inter-sector interference | Not applicable
Simulation assumption | Concatenation of TX-target and target-RX links | The NLOS rays in the STX-SPST link are 1-by-1 randomly coupled with the NLOS rays in the ST-SRX link.
Simulation assumption | The power threshold for path dropping after concatenation for target channel | -40 dB for concatenation
Simulation assumption | CPI length | Companies to report Note: separate discussion for waveform/RS
Simulation assumption | Periodicity of CPI for the same STx/SRx pair | Companies to report
Simulation assumption | Number of CPI within one period for the same STx/SRx pair | Companies to report
Simulation assumption | Overhead of sensing resource | Both Overhead 1 and Overhead 2 ((defined in TR 38.765) shall be reported Overhead 1 (Type_1 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 (Type_1 – Type_2 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 less than 10% is encouraged
Simulation assumption | Selection of sensing Tx-Rx pair | Companies report the option and selection method for STx-SRx pair: Option 1: a same target is modelled in the channel of single Sensing Tx/Rx Option 2: a same target is modelled in the channel of multiple Sensing Txs/Rxs Option 3: a same target is modelled in the channel of all Sensing Txs/Rxs Selection of STx-SRx pair shall be based on achievable measurement information (e.g. RSRP, pathloss, RSRPP, etc.), but not genie-aided information of the target (e.g. location of the target, range between target and STx/SRx, etc.).
Simulation assumption | Tx/Rx beam information, (mt x nt) / (mr x nr) | Companies report the Tx and Rx beam information, of BS and/or UE, when applicable.
Simulation assumption | Algorithm for detection | Companies report the algorithm for detection, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used
Simulation assumption | Algorithm for tracking | Companies report the algorithm for tracking, if applicable, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used
Simulation assumption | Other additional features | Optional Up to companies report (e.g. low power clutter, clutter mobility).

FFS: AGV UE as sensing capable UE

[ON4][H] Proposal 4.2-1d

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Further discuss the sensing measurement/result used to assist communication, which may include, e.g.:

Delay/Doppler/angle/power/phase related information of the ISAC channel

Information of detected objects (static or moving) and/or large size surface (e.g. EO type-2)

Further discuss evaluation of the performance of sensing measurement/results, which are used to assist communication.

Scenario: At least Urban Grid is considered

Urban Grid with or without EO type-2 can both be considered

Other scenarios are not precluded.

FFS detailed evaluation assumptions, sensing modes, prior information of EO-type2, etc.

When evaluated, company should report:

Performance metrics on sensing measurement/result used to assist communication

How such sensing measurement/result is able to assist the communication

The corresponding performance

Further discuss evaluation of communication performance assisted by sensing.

SAC use cases that observed in AI 10.8.2 are considered as starting point, e.g.

Beam management

CSI acquisition

When evaluated, company should use the same scenario and ISAC channel as sensing performance evaluation.

When evaluated, company should clarify whether ideal/non-ideal sensing measurement/result is used to assist communication.

When evaluated, company should report:

Communication-related metric

Detailed methods on how the sensing measurements/results assist the communication

The corresponding performance gain

Note: Strive for minimizing/no extension on existing channel model.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.1-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, for the baseline cases/combinations, the following sensing modes are considered for the baseline cases/combinations.

[TABLE]
Sensing target | Indoor or outdoor | Scenario | Carrier frequency | Sensing mode
UAV | Outdoor | UMa-AV, SMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for UMa-AV only) | TRP mono-static, TRP-TRP bi-static, TRP-UE bi-static (UAV UE?)
Human | Outdoor | UMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static TRP-TRP bi-static
Human | Indoor | Indoor Hotspot | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static UE mono-static, TRP-TRP bi-static
Vehicle | Outdoor | Urban Grid, Highway | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for Urban Grid only) | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static, UE-UE bi-static, TRP mono-static
AGV | Indoor | Indoor factory (SH) | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static TRP mono-static

Note: Other sensing modes (including multi-static sensing) for a baseline case/combination are not precluded.

Note2: Evaluating any sensing mode in this agenda does not necessarily mean any RAN1 specification impact.

Note3: Sensing modes listed in the table may not have the best performance for baseline cases. Encourage companies to improve the performance, e.g. exploring different sensing modes.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, at least the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Evaluate the performance of sensing measurement/results, where the sensing measurement/result may be able to assist communication.

Scenario: Urban Grid with EO type-2

Sensing measurement/result to assist communication, may include, e.g. delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the channel, information of reconstructed wall/building, etc. FFS details.

FFS detailed evaluation assumptions, metric, etc.

Up to company to evaluate and report communication performance assisted by sensing.

Company should at least use realistic sensing measurement/result to assist communication.

Company should use the same scenario and ISAC channel as sensing performance evaluation.

Up to company to report communication-related metric and the corresponding performance gain.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-2a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, further discuss the following potential candidate approaches to assist communication:

Approach 1: Utilize sensing measurement/result to assist the communication performance.

Sensing measurement/result may include, e.g. delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the channel, information of reconstructed wall/building, etc.

Approach 2: Utilize sensing resource/RS to perform communication-related measurement, and then assist the communication performance.

E.g., Perform CSI/beam-related measurement on sensing resource/RS, to assist CSI acquisition and/or beam management.

[FL1][H] Proposal 7.2-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation, for tracking sensing targets/objects, consider the following trajectory model:

Baseline: Straight-line constant velocity trajectory

Other trajectories can also be reported by companies

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.1-1a

For object detection and/or tracking, update the definition of velocity accuracy for 6G ISAC evaluation:

Velocity accuracy is defined as the absolute value of the difference between the estimated velocity and the corresponding true velocity of a sensing target. For single node TRP monostatic sensing or single pair of bistatic sensing, both the radial velocity accuracy and the 3D velocity accuracy can be estimated. The true radial velocity is the projection of true 3D velocity on the direction from TRP to target. the sum of the following two components:

Component 1: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to STX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the STX;

Component 2: The projection of the velocity difference vector on the unit-norm vector from target to SRX, where the velocity difference vector is between the true 3D velocity of the target and the true 3D velocity of the SRX.

Note: If STx and SRx are static, true radial velocity is , where is the true 3D velocity of the target, is the bistatic angle between incident direction and scattering direction, and is the angle between the true 3D velocity vector and the bisector vector within the bistatic angle . For mono-static sensing, .

[FL1][M] Proposal 6.2-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, for object detection and/or tracking, regarding the performance target in UMa and Indoor Factory scenarios given by RAN Plenary:

The performance objective on False Alarm Probability (5%) applies to both False Alarm Probability Type 1 and False Alarm Probability Type 2.

The vertical localization accuracy requirement is only applicable to the case of sensing UAV.

Simulation assumption | Adjacent channel interference | Option 1: Not modelled Option 2: Explicitly modelled Including interference from communication signal, if applicable. | Option 1: Not modelled Option 2: Explicitly modelled Including interference from communication signal, if applicable.
Simulation assumption | Concatenation of TX-target and target-RX links | Option 1 (Optional): Each NLOS ray in the STX-SPST link is coupled with each NLOS ray in the SPST-SRX link Option 2: The NLOS rays in the STX-SPST link are 1-by-1 randomly coupled with the NLOS rays in the ST-SRX link. | Option 1 (Optional): Each NLOS ray in the STX-SPST link is coupled with each NLOS ray in the SPST-SRX link Option 2: The NLOS rays in the STX-SPST link are 1-by-1 randomly coupled with the NLOS rays in the ST-SRX link.
Simulation assumption | The power threshold for path dropping after concatenation for target channel | -25dB and -40dB are respectively used for Option 1 and Option 2 for concatenation | -25dB and -40dB are respectively used for Option 1 and Option 2 for concatenation
Simulation assumption | CPI length | Companies to report | Companies to report
Simulation assumption | Periodicity of CPI for the same STx/SRx pair | Companies to report | Companies to report
Simulation assumption | Number of CPI within one period for the same STx/SRx pair | Companies to report | Companies to report
Simulation assumption | Overhead of sensing resource | Both Overhead 1 and Overhead 2 ((defined in TR 38.765) shall be reported Overhead 1 (Type_1 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 (Type_1 – Type_2 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 less than 10% is encouraged | Both Overhead 1 and Overhead 2 ((defined in TR 38.765) shall be reported Overhead 1 (Type_1 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 (Type_1 – Type_2 + Type_3) over all radio DL and UL resources Overhead 2 less than 10% is encouraged
Simulation assumption | Selection of sensing Tx-Rx pair | Option 1: a same target is modelled in the channel of single Sensing Tx/Rx Option 2: a same target is modelled in the channel of multiple Sensing Txs/Rxs Option 3: a same target is modelled in the channel of all Sensing Txs/Rxs Note: Selection of STx-SRx pair shall be based on achievable measurement information (e.g. RSRP, pathloss, RSRPP, etc.), but not genie-aided information (e.g. location of the target, range between target and STx/SRx, etc.). | Option 1: a same target is modelled in the channel of single Sensing Tx/Rx Option 2: a same target is modelled in the channel of multiple Sensing Txs/Rxs Option 3: a same target is modelled in the channel of all Sensing Txs/Rxs Note: Selection of STx-SRx pair shall be based on achievable measurement information (e.g. RSRP, pathloss, RSRPP, etc.), but not genie-aided information (e.g. location of the target, range between target and STx/SRx, etc.).
Simulation assumption | Tx/Rx beam information, (mt x nt) / (mr x nr) | Companies report the Tx and Rx beam information, of BS and/or UE, when applicable. | Companies report the Tx and Rx beam information, of BS and/or UE, when applicable.
Simulation assumption | Algorithm for detection | Companies report the algorithm for detection, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used | Companies report the algorithm for detection, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used
Simulation assumption | Algorithm for tracking | Companies report the algorithm for tracking, if applicable, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used | Companies report the algorithm for tracking, if applicable, including whether/how fusion of sensing data from multiple SRx is used
Simulation assumption | Other additional features | Optional Up to companies report (e.g. low power clutter, clutter mobility). | Optional Up to companies report (e.g. low power clutter, clutter mobility).

Scope of evaluation

[TABLE]

Several companies propose to confirm the working assumption as it is, while some other companies additionally propose updates especially for communication assistance part.

Note that, multi-static can be constructed by one or more sensing modes with more than one STx and/or SRx. It is not precluded anyway.

In addition, Nokia proposes to remove 30 GHz for UMa and SMa, and Ofinno would like to consider 30 GHz as optional. FL thinks individual companies can choose the evaluated frequency but no need to prohibit an agreed combination. Panasonic suggests mapping different baseline combinations into cases in TR 22.837 and TR 22.870. FL thinks this does not change our evaluation scope with or without the mapping. Some other companies would like to modify the table, e.g. adding scenarios. Please refrain from such up-scoping at this stage due to the limited time.

Finally, the following proposal is made, based on the input. Some notes are also added to (1) clarify the intension, and (2) keep the door open for other sensing modes.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.1-1

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation of object detection and/or tracking, the following sensing modes are considered for the baseline cases/combinations.

[TABLE]
Sensing target | Indoor or outdoor | Scenario | Carrier frequency | Sensing mode
UAV | Outdoor | UMa-AV, SMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for UMa-AV only) | TRP mono-static, TRP-TRP bi-static
Human | Outdoor | UMa | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static
Human | Indoor | Indoor Hotspot | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static
Vehicle | Outdoor | Urban Grid, Highway | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz (for Urban Grid only) | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static
AGV | Indoor | Indoor factory (SH) | 4 GHz, ~7 GHz, 30 GHz | TRP-UE bi-static, UE-TRP bi-static

Note: Other sensing modes (including multi-static sensing) for a baseline case/combination are not precluded.

Note2: Evaluating any sensing mode in this agenda does not necessarily mean any RAN1 specification impact.

Qualcomm | Qualcomm | No | No | We don’t agree with the effort to explicitly limit to specific sensing modes the evaluation and we believe this table will be used out of context for downscoping specific sensing modes. We also observe that TRP-UE has been included in multiple outdoor and indoor scenarios. What will we learn additionally by having the same sensing mode in multiple scenarios? TRP-UE and UE-TRP have a lot of similarities; mainly the Tx power is different; the rest are the same. Instead of having both modes everywhere, at least for one of scenarios UE monostatic should be included. In the above list, it appears as if the TRP-UE/UE-TRP are the most important scenarios, however, from our understanding, these sensing mode suffers the most from bad LOS condition and will have limited performance in many cases. TRP-based sensing modes or UE-monostatic will have much better LOS conditions in several of these deployments.

And we also provide initial simulation results for highway and urban grid scenario, as cited below, which shows that UE-UE bistatic sensing and UE monostatic sensing are more suitable for urban grid scenario. and also have comparable performance to the TRP-UE bistatic and UE-TRP bistatic sensing for highway scenario. Table 3: Evaluation results for RMa-Highway[R1-2603924] Table 4: Evaluation results for Urban grid[R1-2603924] For human outdoor sensing, similar issues are suffered since same LOS/NLOS formulas are used for TRP-target link in UMa scenario and urban grid scenario. Therefore, we propose to consider UE-UE bistatic sensing and UE mono-static sensing as baseline sensing mode for automotive sensing and human outdoor sensing. | At least for Urban Grid scenario, we do not see UE-TRP bi-static and TRP-UE bi-static sensing modes can be considered as a good starting point for evaluation, due to the low LOS probability between TRP and vehicle as we commented in the last meeting, which has a significantly negative impact on the sensing performance. And we also provide initial simulation results for highway and urban grid scenario, as cited below, which shows that UE-UE bistatic sensing and UE monostatic sensing are more suitable for urban grid scenario. and also have comparable performance to the TRP-UE bistatic and UE-TRP bistatic sensing for highway scenario. Table 3: Evaluation results for RMa-Highway[R1-2603924] Table 4: Evaluation results for Urban grid[R1-2603924] For human outdoor sensing, similar issues are suffered since same LOS/NLOS formulas are used for TRP-target link in UMa scenario and urban grid scenario. Therefore, we propose to consider UE-UE bistatic sensing and UE mono-static sensing as baseline sensing mode for automotive sensing and human outdoor sensing.

Communication assistance

Views on communication assistance from companies in RAN1#125 are summarized below.

Necessity of evaluating communication assistance

Evaluate sensing (e.g. environment, blocker, target) only: CMCC (1st), Samsung, Huawei, DOCOMO, Ericsson, Ofinno, [Panasonic],

Evaluate communication only (i.e. assuming ideal sensing result): vivo, ZTE, AT&T, Qualcomm,

Evaluate both sensing and communication: CMCC (2nd), AT&T, [Qualcomm], LGE, Google, Apple, T-Mobile USA, SK Telecom,

Up to companies report: xiaomi, Samsung (communication part), DOCOMO (communication part)

Deprioritize: Interdigital, ETRI, [LGE], Sony,

Use cases of communication assistance for evaluation

[TABLE]
Use case | What to sense | What/how to assist | Companies
Not limited | Detected channel multipath where wall/building exist nearby | Extract and reconstruct channel information, to generate hypothetical channel for communication purpose. Detailed usage is not limited. | Huawei
Not limited | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) | Up to vendors/operators. Detailed usage is not limited. | DOCOMO, Samsung, LGE, Ericsson, [Panasonic],
Beam management | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) | Assist beam prediction By using multipath channel information (e.g. angle, delay, power) of a UE from reconstructed wall/building By pre-identify static blocker such as wall/building | Vivo*, CMCC, [AT&T], Qualcomm, Google, T-Mobile USA, Sony, Lenovo, Tejas, NEC, China Telecom, Ofinno,
Beam management | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) | Assist beam forming Buildings are treated as virtual transmitters, where reflected or scattered paths are intentionally beamformed to reach NLOS UE | LGE, NIST,
Beam management | Blockers (e.g. vehicle) | Assis beam prediction By pre-identify dynamic blocker such as vehicle | CMCC, Apple, China Telecom,

Beam management | None | Assist beam selection By using sensing resource to measure L1-RSRP and select communication beams | ZTE

CSI acquisition/prediction | None | Assist CSI acquisition By measuring CSI from sensing resource | ZTE

Mobility management | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) Blockers (e.g. vehicle) | Assist handover reliability By using target cell's channel conditions based on the UE's trajectory and the reconstructed wall/building | LGE, [CMCC], [Qualcomm], T-Mobile USA, Sony, Lenovo, NEC, China Telecom, Ofinno,
Positioning enhancement | Environment (e.g. Wall, building) Blockers (e.g. vehicle) | Assist positioning accuracy By providing reliable anchors for NLOS mitigation and multipath-assisted positioning, from reconstructed wall/building | [CMCC], [Qualcomm]

Definition of sensing metric

[TABLE]
What to sense | Metric | Companies

Wall, building | Reconstruction accuracy Accuracy of position, resolution, etc., of the reconstructed environment objects | Samsung (coarse), DOCOMO, LGE, Google, Ericsson(2nd), T-Mobile USA, Lenovo, NEC, Ofinno,
Wall, building | Reconstruction accuracy Chamfer distance, Hausdorff distance, or Precision and Recall between reconstructed and ground truth EO | AT&T
Wall, building | Reconstruction accuracy Specular reflection points estimation accuracy | Ericsson (1st)
Wall, building | Detection probability, False Alarm probability, of the dropped wall/building | Samsung, T-Mobile USA,
Detected path from the environment | RMSE of power, delay and angle, between hypothesized channel and ground true channel | Huawei
Moving/static objects, e.g. vehicle, human that holding UE | (Same as object detection/tracking), Detection probability, False alarm probability, location/velocity accuracy | Ericsson, Nokia, T-Mobile USA, Lenovo,

Evaluation methodology

Proponents (e.g. Huawei, vivo, ZTE, Qualcomm, Apple, T-Mobile USA, Lenovo, NEC, etc.) propose updated methodologies in different ways explicitly or implicitly. And, depending on ‘how to assist communication’, different methodologies may appear (well summarized by Lenovo, see R1-2604242).

Approach 1: Assist communication by Prior information of Environment

An example from Huawei is copied below (slightly modified). Note that no sensing target (e.g. vehicle) is needed in this example.

[TABLE]
The high-level evaluation processing flow can be diagramed as follows: Deployment and scenario: Set up the simulation configuration, including the layout, sensing Tx/Rx node, and channel model. Sensing measurement: Perform sensing measurement and sensing signal processing to acquire the sensing measurement results. For an instance, the detected paths are extracted. Channel hypothesizing: Reconstruct the channel information e.g., the RSRP and multi-path component based on the sensing measurement results. For example, based on the sensing measurement results, the propagation channel can be regenerated by the same channel model and hypothesized for the Tx-Rx link. Communication assistance: Perform the assisting communication procedure and the details depend on the use cases to be assisted.

Approach 2: Assist communication by sensing the attached object (e.g. human) of UE

Lenovo: New methodology may include: define co-location of a passive target and a dropped UE according to the UE type

Approach 3: Assist communication by using sensing signal/resource

Lenovo: New methodology may include: define communication evaluation setup given sensing signal transmissions

Evaluation scenario

Urban grid: CMCC, vivo, Huawei, ZTE, Samsung, [DOCOMO], Ericsson

UMa: vivo

Depending on methodology: Lenovo

Others

No simultaneous/mixed sensing + communication evaluation: Ericsson, CATT/CICTCI,

Further discuss: Nokia, SK Telecom, Apple

Summary

FL: Companies’ views are SO Divergent on Everything😄. Having said this, we need to figure out what can we do and whether/what/how to do, with limited time.

Let’s start discussion to have better understanding with each other from high-level. Further details, like use case, metric, performance target, channel model extension… can be discussed later once the evaluation scope is clear.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-1

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, further discuss the following potential candidate SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scopes:

SAC Scope 0: No dedicated evaluation for communication assistance.

Note: Scope 0 can be considered as special case of Scope 1, assuming the communication can be assisted by sensing moving objects like vehicle, etc., which is already included in the study of (moving) object detection and/or tracking.

SAC Scope 1: Only evaluate sensing, which can potentially assist communication.

SAC Scope 1a: Evaluate sensing which can potentially assist communication. Evaluation and report of any communication performance or performance gain is up to companies.

SAC Scope 2: Evaluate communication performance with ideal sensing assistance.

SAC Scope 2a: Evaluate communication performance gain between ‘with ideal sensing assistance’ and ‘without any sensing assistance’.

SAC Scope 3: Evaluate communication performance with realistic sensing assistance.

SAC Scope 3a: Evaluate communication performance gain between ‘with realistic sensing assistance’ and ‘without any sensing assistance’.

SAC Scope 3b: Evaluate communication performance gain and loss between ‘with realistic sensing assistance’, ‘without any sensing assistance’ and ‘with ideal sensing assistance’.

NEC | Yes | Yes | Scope 1 is preferred for now.
DCM |  |  | Considering limited TU and divergent views from companies, we are OK with Scope 0 or 1.
Huawei, HiSilicon | Ok to further discuss | Ok to further discuss | In general, regarding sensing assisting comm., we should at least note that what sensing aspect can assist comm should be at least be studied, assuming the comm can be assisted by sensing moving objects is not the right way to go at the beginning of the study since it has been commonly acknowledged that what can assist comm is the stable and dominant paths, which clearly are not the paths caused by the moving objects. We are open to further discuss how to evaluate and hopefully we can figure out a more pragmatic approach for the evaluations.
Apple |  |  | Limit to maximum Scope 2.
PCL | Yes | Yes | We support starting with SAC Scope 2a (evaluate communication performance gain with ideal sensing assistance) as a practical and focused baseline. This allows the group to first quantify the upper bound of potential benefit that sensing can bring to communication, without the added complexity of realistic sensing imperfections. Once the value is demonstrated under ideal conditions, the group can consider whether and how to introduce realistic sensing assumptions (Scope 3a/3b) in future releases.
CMCC |  |  | We support SAC Scope 1 (specifically Scope 1a) as the primary baseline for the early evaluation phase, and we do not oppose SAC Scope 3 (Scope 3a/3b) as a subsequent or optional step. However, we have reservations regarding SAC Scope 0 and Scope 2. Our detailed rationale is as follows: Support for Scope 1 / 1a (Priority): As detailed in our contribution (R1-2604027), simulating closed-loop, end-to-end communication metrics (e.g., tracking a dynamic blocker and executing proactive MAC/RRC switching) in SLS is notoriously resource-intensive and computationally prohibitive at this early stage. Evaluating realistic sensing metrics (e.g., estimation accuracy of AoA/Delay of reflectors, or blockage prediction probability) serves as a highly reliable and efficient proxy for communication gains. Scope 1a provides the best balance between standard progression speed and evaluation meaningfulness. Open to Scope 3: We do not oppose Scope 3, as it reflects the ultimate closed-loop performance. However, considering the heavy simulation burden, it should be treated as a secondary priority or left up to companies' capabilities (aligning with the spirit of Scope 1a). Against Scope 0 (No dedicated evaluation): It is widely acknowledged by both academia and industry that sensing can significantly assist communication. However, simply reusing the generic "moving object tracking" evaluations (as suggested in Scope 0) completely misses the core objective of SAC. SAC requires specific channel mapping mechanisms (e.g., tracking dynamic blockers or static dominant reflectors) that are entirely different from traditional UAV/vehicle tracking. Therefore, a dedicated evaluation scope for SAC is indispensable. Against Scope 2 (Ideal sensing assistance): Evaluating communication with "ideal sensing" brings little value to RAN1 physical-layer standardization. The fundamental purpose of the SAC evaluation is to answer the question: "How much sensing accuracy translates to how much communication gain?" We need to evaluate the sensitivity of communication performance to realistic sensing errors (e.g., how AoA/Delay estimation fluctuations impact proactive beam switching). Scope 2 completely ignores this trade-off, which defeats the purpose of designing robust ISAC reference signals.
ETRI |  |  | Scope 1 is preferred.
Sony | Comments | Comments | We are ok with Scope 0 or 1.
LGE | Comment | Comment | We prefer SAC Scope 0 at this stage. Considering the limited time for Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation, RAN1 should first focus on object detection and/or tracking. Since the 6G communication system and relevant assistance procedures are still under discussion, SAC performance evaluation seems premature for Rel-20 SI. If needed, SAC Scope 1A could be considered, where sensing measurements/results are evaluated while communication performance or performance gain is left up to companies.
NIST | Comment | Comment | To study sensing-assisted communication, both sensing and communication performance need to be studied. The sensing aspect of the evaluation should prioritize estimating Multipath Components (MPCs) to generate and maintain an RF channel knowledge map of the environment.
Nokia |  |  | We prefer to discuss SAC scope 3.
Spreadtrum | Comment | Comment | Before down-scoping, we suggest to firstly recognize and confirm the content of each scope.
CATT/CICTCI | Yes | Yes | Agree to further discuss the above scopes, and our preference is SAC Scope1. As FL mentioned, companies’ views on how to evaluate SAC are still diverging. Given the limited time of R20 6GR ISAC, Scope 1 can be a good attempt when considering potential assistance from sensing.
Huawei, HiSilicon | Suggestion | Suggestion | We have been thinking how to make a more simplified and friendlier approach for most of companies to do the evaluations, how about the following one? For the study on sensing assisting evaluation, as the first step, evaluate the channel similarity between the channel extracted from sensing measurements and the ground truth channel The ground truth channel is the channel generated by ISAC channel model. The channel similarity can consider: Similarity of the detected path, e.g., RMSE Similarity of the channel matrix H, e.g., RMSE, SGCS.

FL: Based on the input, SAC Scope 1a seems to be a possible compromise direction for the time being. The following proposal is made for Tuesday offline discussion.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-1a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, at least the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Evaluate the sensing performance where the sensing measurement/result may be able to assist communication.

Scenario: Urban Grid with EO type-2

FFS detailed evaluation assumptions, metric, etc.

Up to company to evaluate and report communication performance assisted by sensing.

Company should at least use realistic sensing measurement/result to assist communication.

Company should use the same scenario and ISAC channel as sensing performance evaluation.

Up to company to report communication-related metric and the performance gain.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-2

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, further discuss the following potential candidate approaches to assist communication:

Approach 1: Utilize sensing measurement/result of environment (e.g. wall/building, static vehicles, moving vehicles, etc.) to assist communication (e.g. beam management, CSI acquisition/prediction, etc.) of UE(s) in/near the environment.

E.g., Sense and reconstruct the wall/building, utilize reconstructed wall/building to obtain hypothesized wireless channel, to assist the beam management, CSI acquisition/prediction… of UEs.

Approach 2: Utilize sensing measurement/result of sensing target(s)/object(s) that carrying UE(s), to assist the communication performance of the UE(s).

E.g., Sense the trajectory of a human that carrying a UE, to assist the beam management, mobility… of this UE.

Approach 3: Utilize sensing resource/RS to perform communication-related measurement, and then assist the communication performance of UE(s).

E.g., Perform CSI/beam-related measurement on sensing resource/RS, to assist CSI acquisition and/or beam management.

[TABLE]

vivo | Yes | Approach 1 and Approach 2 may have not enough difference between each other. The sensing measurement/result of Approach 1 and Approach 2 is impacted by both the environment and the sensing target(s)/object(s) that carrying UE(s). Therefore, Approach is the baseline for discussion and Approach 2 can provide some additional sensing measurements, e.g., the trajectory of UE. Approach 3 is on another page (it is communication-related measurement, not sensing related measurement of Approach 1 and 2) and can be discussed separately. We suggest that the sensing measurements for Approach 1/2 should also include the delay/angle/doppler/power of the detected path(s), i.e., sensing measurement level C.

CMCC |  | Regarding the proposed approaches, we believe that Approach 1 and Approach 2 are intrinsically intertwined and inseparable, particularly for TRP-UE and UE-TRP sensing modes. Furthermore, we suggest adopting a use-case-driven methodology rather than prematurely debating abstract categories. Our detailed views are as follows: The Inseparability of Approach 1 and Approach 2: Effective communication assistance (SAC) inherently relies on the spatial-temporal correlation between the UE and its propagation environment. Knowing only the environment (Approach 1) is meaningless without knowing the target UE's trajectory (Approach 2), and vice versa. For example, as detailed in our contribution, for CSI Prediction, the network must simultaneously track the location/trajectory of the UE (Approach 2) and the geometry of the dominant reflectors (Approach 1) to mathematically predict the channel clusters. Therefore, treating them as mutually exclusive or separate candidate approaches is physically disconnected from reality. Prioritize Concrete Use Cases over Abstract Approaches: In the early stage of 6G ISAC evaluation, RAN1 should focus on specific, concrete SAC use cases that already have relatively mature evaluation methodologies in traditional communication studies. Currently, the industry consensus primarily revolves around concrete procedures such as Beam Management (e.g., proactive blockage avoidance) and CSI Acquisition/Prediction (e.g., pilot overhead reduction). Suggested Way Forward: We suggest that RAN1 should first down-select and agree on the concrete use cases (we highly recommend Beam Management and CSI Prediction as the starting points). Once the specific use cases are agreed upon, the required sensing approaches (whether tracking a blocker, a reflector, or the UE itself) will naturally emerge and be defined. Abstract categorization of "Approaches" should be deferred and summarized later when a comprehensive set of feasible use cases is fully stabilized.
Sony | No | The scope is still unclear at this stage. We can come back on this later
LGE |  | We prefer Approach 1. However, the SAC evaluation scope should be clarified first before discussing detailed approaches.
NIST | Yes in general | There are some overlaps between moving environmental objects and moving sensing targets.
Nokia | No | Approach 1 is okay. Approach 2 is not clear on how to associate a target with a UE. This issue should be addressed first before any study. Approach 3 is out of the scope of the 6G sensing study.
Spreadtrum | No | We suggest to discuss the SAC evaluation scopes firstly.
CATT/CICTCI | Yes | Agree to further study.

FL: Based on the input, the following proposal is made for Tuesday offline discussion.

[FL1][H] Proposal 4.2-2a

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, further discuss the following potential candidate approaches to assist communication:

Approach 1: Utilize sensing measurement/result to assist the communication performance.

Sensing measurement/result may include, e.g. delay/Doppler/angle/power related information of the channel, information of reconstructed wall/building, etc.

Approach 2: Utilize sensing resource/RS to perform communication-related measurement, and then assist the communication performance.

E.g., Perform CSI/beam-related measurement on sensing resource/RS, to assist CSI acquisition and/or beam management.

[FL2][H] Proposal 4.2-1b

For Rel-20 6G ISAC evaluation on communication assistance, at least the following SAC (sensing assisted communication) evaluation scope is considered:

Autonomous driving: IIT Kanpur

FL | Considering the limited TU, it is strongly recommended focusing on (1) object detection and/or tracking and (2) communication assistance.

Evaluation assumption

Evaluation methodology

The following evaluation methodology was agreed for Rel-19 5G-A ISAC evaluation and captured in TR 38.765 [5].

[TABLE]
6.1 Evaluation methodologies The following general procedure for performance evaluation of NR ISAC is provided. 1) Simulation parameter configuration 2) Sensing scenario generation, including the deployment of sensing Tx/Rx (STXs/SRXs) 3) Dropping N target(s), where N is equal to 0 or larger than 0 4) Channel generation and STX/SRX determination for the targets 5) Sensing signal generation and passing the sensing signal to the generated channel 6) Sensing signal processing at each SRX, optionally, sensing signal processing based on fusion from multiple STXs/SRXs - E.g., optionally, sensing signal processing based on tracking 7) Sensing performance metric calculation.

Evaluation methodology for object detection and/or tracking

Some companies (e.g. Qualcomm, DOCOMO) think the existing methodology can serve as baseline. InterDigital proposes a common methodology for 6G sensing regarding many aspects of evaluation assumption, which have similar guidance but more detailed steps.

Vivo proposes new methodology of dropping UEs and sensing targets, considering range constraint between target and UE when UE is involved in sensing.

[TABLE]
FL | In FL’s understanding, the evaluation methodology seems general enough to support both object detection and tracking. For example, it already mentions ‘sensing signal processing based on tracking’. Note that with this evaluation methodology in Rel-20 NR ISAC: Though most companies evaluated object detection, some companies already simulated tracking (e.g. ZTE (R1-2600973), CMCC (R1-2600381)). Many companies already adopted sensing data fusion from different TRPs, without explicitly mentioning this in the evaluation methodology. Based on the discussion in RAN1#124bis, most companies think that evaluation methodology in 5G-A ISAC is sufficient for object detection and/or tracking for Rel-20 6G ISAC [6]. Thus this issue will be opened only if significant change is identified.

Evaluation methodology for communication assistance

(TBD. Depend on the progress. See section 4.2)

Evaluation assumptions of baseline cases/combinations

Based on Rel-19 channel model study, Rel-20 NR ISAC evaluation, Rel-20 6G communication section, and companies’ input to this agenda in this meeting, the following evaluation assumptions for baseline cases/combinations are generated.

[FL1][M] Proposal 5.2-1

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, adopt the table above as the evaluation assumption for baseline case/combination of sensing UAV.

[TABLE]
Company | Yes or No | Comments
Tiami | Yes | 
IDCC | Comment | We support this proposal in principle. For baseline UAV dropping in vertical plane, we prefer “uniform distribution between 1.5 m to 300 m”. Dropping UAV targets below 25 m allow us to evaluate UAV sensing in low altitude. This is well representative of emerging low altitude use cases (e.g., drone delivery).
vivo | Yes | 
OPPO | Yes | 
Cohere | Yes | 
ZTE | Comment | Based on the proposal 4.1-1, TRP mono-static is assumed as one of baseline cases. Therefore, at least BS antenna configuration and BS transmitting power should at least consider the effect of TRP mono-static sensing mode. Otherwise, such evaluation assumption would provide too much limited information. Maybe it would be better if we finish discussing on revision for mono-static sensing mode and come back then for the evaluation assumption for baseline cases of UAV. What’s more, UE configurations are not needed for UAV use case if UE related sensing mode is not baseline.
Qualcomm | Comment | On the BS antenna configuration for 4 GHz UMa, we think we should use the same as in 5G-A ISAC study, which is the one shown in yellow now in the table, but we would like to keep it. (M, N, P, Mg, Ng; Mp, Np) for 4GHz, 4.9GHz - Configuration A - Tx: (8,8,2,1,1;4,8) - Rx: (8,8,2,1,1;4,8)
Xiaomi | [Yes] | For UMa-AV scenario: BS transmit power (per 20 MHz): The BS transmit power is suitable for non TRP-mono cases, the parameter name can be updated with “BS transmit power (per 20 MHz) for non TRP mono-static”. Besides, 49 dBm per 20MHz is preferred to achieve maximum power of 56dBm for 4GHz with 100MHz. As for the note, the descriptions for indoor can be removed since UMa-AV is outdoor only. BS antenna configuration: So many candidate options (2 or 3 for one candidate frequency) are not suitable for results comparison. One candidate value can used as baseline, others are optional. “Outdoor Combination 1” for 4GHz, “Outdoor Combination 1” for around 7Ghz, and “Outdoor Combination 1” for 30GHz can be used as baseline. As for the note, it could be revised after discussion of Proposal 5.3-1 UE antenna configuration: For Handheld UE, config 1 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz For CPE UE, config 0 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz. The Tx antenna configuration of UE should also be defined. UE antenna radiation pattern: For Handheld UE, Table 7.3-2 in TR 38.901 is assumed for antenna config with Alt 2 and isotropic radiation power pattern is assumed for Alt1, as agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda. For CPE UE, three candidate radiation pattern is agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda, maybe one can be selected as baseline. UE array orientation: For handheld UE, random in horizontal domain could be used. UE distribution: One candidate value of N could be selected as baseline. For example, N = 30 for handheld UE, and N = 30 for CPE UE. UE noise figure: One candidate value of noise figure could be selected as baseline. For example, 9dB for around 7GHz and below and 10dB for around 15GHz and above, which is usually used in NR communication evaluation. Physical information and RCS: UAV with small size could be used as baseline. Additional parameters: The minimum BS-UE distance and minimum UE-UE distance could also be defined. The minimum BS-UE 2D distance is 35m and minimum UE-UE 2D distance is 1m
NEC | Yes | 
DCM | Yes | 
Huawei, HiSilicon | Comment | Thanks to FL for the efforts to compact all the assumptions together. A few comments for further consideration/discussion: Regarding BS antenna config for UAV, we prefer to keep the same config as agreed/simulated in 5GA. Otherwise, companies need to re-run the simulations just because of the BS configuration change. Regarding UE antenna config for 7GHz, do we really need to set baseline and optional. For bandwidth, we can consider 400MHz since it is supported for comm as well.

[FL1][M] Proposal 5.2-2

For Rel-20 6G ISAC performance evaluation, adopt the table above as the evaluation assumption for baseline case/combination of sensing human.

[TABLE]
Company | Yes or No | Comments
Tiami | Yes | 
IDCC | Yes | 
vivo | No | Regarding the dropping of sensing targets, we propose that the supported optional number of targets includes the following values: N = 1, 2, 10, 15. Furthermore, for multi‑UE cooperative sensing, multiple UEs can be dropped to cover the entire topology. For single‑UE sensing, i.e., when sensing results from multiple UEs are not fused, the UE should not be required to sense all the targets across the entire topology or targets too far from that UE. Therefore, at least for single‑UE sensing scenarios, we propose introducing a distance constraint between the target and the UE when dropping targets; i.e., only targets within a certain distance range are required to be sensed. Regarding the UE antenna configuration, we propose to add the configuration with (M, N, P, Mg, Ng, Mp, Np) = (1, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1, 2) for dual polarization. In addition, we suggest that the number of UEs should include more values, e.g., N = 1, 10, 15, 20, 30, 60, 120, or be left up to company report.
OPPO | Yes | 
Qualcomm | Comment | We also supportive to introduce N=1
Xiaomi | [Yes] | For UMa scenario: BS transmit power (per 20 MHz): The BS transmit power is suitable for non TRP-mono cases, the parameter name can be updated with “BS transmit power (per 20 MHz) for non TRP mono-static”. Besides, 49 dBm per 20MHz is preferred to achieve maximum power of 56dBm for 4GHz with 100MHz. As for the note, the descriptions for indoor can be removed since UMa is outdoor only. BS antenna configuration: Since the table is assumptions for baseline, so many options (2 or 3 for one candidate frequency) are not suitable for results comparison. One candidate value can used as baseline, others are optional. “Outdoor Combination 1” for 4GHz, “Outdoor Combination 1” for around 7Ghz, and “Outdoor Combination 1” for 30GHz can be used as baseline. As for the note, it could be revised after discussion of Proposal 5.3-1 UE antenna configuration: For Handheld UE, config 1 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz For CPE UE, config 0 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz. The Tx antenna configuration of UE should also be defined. UE antenna radiation pattern: For Handheld UE, Table 7.3-2 in TR 38.901 is assumed for antenna config with Alt 2 and isotropic radiation power pattern is assumed for Alt1, as agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda. For CPE UE, three candidate radiation pattern is agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda, maybe one can be selected as baseline. UE array orientation: For handheld UE, random in horizontal domain could be used. UE distribution: One candidate value of N could be selected as baseline. For example, N = 30 for handheld UE, and N = 30 for CPE UE. UE noise figure: One candidate value of noise figure could be selected as baseline. For example, 9dB for around 7GHz and below and 10dB for around 15GHz and above, which is usually used in NR communication evaluation. Mobility: Option 1: 3km/h could be selected as the baseline. Minimum 3D distance between STx/SRx and sensing target: To cover all the sensing modes, it could be updated as: The minimum 2D distances between TRP and the sensing target is 35 m. The minimum 2D distances between UE and the sensing target is 1 m. Minimum target-target 3D distance: Option 2 can be used as baseline. Physical information and RCS: Option 2 for RCS model could be used as baseline. Additional parameters: The minimum BS-UE distance and minimum UE-UE distance could also be defined. The minimum BS-UE 2D distance is 35m and minimum UE-UE 2D distance is 1m. For Indoor hotspot scenario: Scenario: The Indoor - Open office could be used as baseline. BS transmit power (per 20 MHz): For the note, the descriptions for outdoor can be removed since InH is indoor. BS antenna configuration: So many candidate options (2 for one candidate frequency) are not suitable for results comparison. One candidate value can used as baseline, others are optional. “Indoor Combination 1” for 4GHz, “Indoor Combination 1” for around 7Ghz, and “Indoor Combination 1” for 30GHz can be used as baseline. As for the note, it could be revised after discussion of Proposal 5.3-1 UE antenna height: 1.5m could be used for handheld UE UE antenna configuration: For Handheld UE, config 1 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz For CPE UE, config 0 could be selected as baseline for 30GHz. The Tx antenna configuration of UE should also be defined. UE antenna radiation pattern: For Handheld UE, Table 7.3-2 in TR 38.901 is assumed for antenna config with Alt 2 and isotropic radiation power pattern is assumed for Alt1, as agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda. For CPE UE, three candidate radiation pattern is agreed in communication evaluation assumption agenda, maybe one can be selected as baseline. UE array orientation: For handheld UE, random in horizontal domain could be used. UE distribution: One candidate value of N could be selected as baseline. For example, N = 30 for handheld UE, and N = 30 for CPE UE. UE noise figure: One candidate value of noise figure could be selected as baseline. For example, 9dB for around 7GHz and below and 10dB for around 15GHz and above, which is usually used in NR communication evaluation. Mobility: Option 1: 3km/h could be selected as the baseline. Minimum 3D distance between STx/SRx and sensing target: To cover all the sensing modes, it could be updated as: The minimum 2D distances between TRP and the sensing target is 35 m. The minimum 2D distances between UE and the sensing target is 1 m. Minimum target-target 3D distance: Option 2 can be used as baseline. Physical information and RCS: Option 2 for RCS model could be used as baseline. Additional parameters: The minimum BS-UE distance and minimum UE-UE distance could also be defined. The minimum BS-UE 2D distance is 0m and minimum UE-UE 2D distance is 1m.
